VADIP plans — offered through Delta Dental and MetLife — cover a full range of services including diagnostics, preventive care, root canals, surgery, and emergency dental care. Veterans and eligible family members enroll directly through the chosen insurer's website.

konular.baslik: VA Prescription Refills and Medication Management
konular.ozet: Veterans enrolled in VA health care can refill and track VA prescriptions online through My HealtheVet on VA.gov, and view their complete medication list in one place. Eligibility for online prescription management requires an active VA health care enrollment and at least one prescription filled through a VA pharmacy. Support is available Monday–Friday, 8 a.m.–8 p.m. ET by phone, or 24/7 for general VA.gov account questions.

Veterans who owe money to VA — for example, due to education benefit overpayments or health care copay bills — can make payments online at Pay.va.gov or Pay.gov, by phone, by mail, or in person. Those experiencing financial hardship may request a debt waiver within one year of receiving the first debt notice, or dispute a charge within 30 days to pause collection actions. VA encourages veterans to contact the Debt Management Center promptly to explore repayment plans or relief options before debt is referred to collections.

Veterans who participated in atmospheric nuclear testing (1945–1962), occupied Hiroshima or Nagasaki after WWII, worked at gaseous diffusion plants for 250 or more days before 1992, or served at underground test sites may qualify for presumptive VA disability compensation for radiation-related cancers including leukemia, lymphomas, and cancers of the brain, breast, colon, thyroid, and several other sites. Eligible veterans also qualify for a free Ionizing Radiation Registry health exam through VA and receive care under Priority Group 6 with no copays for radiation-related conditions. File a claim at VA.gov and contact VA's Ionizing Radiation program for more information.

The Edith Nourse Rogers STEM Scholarship provides eligible veterans and dependents with up to 9 months of additional GI Bill benefits — or up to $30,000, whichever is less — to help complete undergraduate STEM degrees or healthcare training programs. Applicants must be using the Post-9/11 GI Bill or Fry Scholarship and have 6 months or fewer of GI Bill entitlement remaining. Apply online through VA.gov while still enrolled in an approved STEM program.

konular.baslik: VET TEC 2.0 — High-Tech Training for Veterans
konular.ozet: VET TEC 2.0 helps eligible veterans and active-duty service members build skills for careers in technology fields including computer programming, software development, data processing, information sciences, and media applications, with VA covering tuition, a housing allowance, and books and supplies. Unlike the original VET TEC, this program requires at least 36 months of active-duty service and applicants must be under age 62. Enrollment is limited to 4,000 participants per year; apply through VA.gov.

VA's Whole Health program focuses on what matters most to each veteran rather than solely on diagnosis and treatment, offering personalized health plans built around individual values and goals. Enrolled veterans can access complementary and integrative services including acupuncture, biofeedback, clinical hypnosis, guided imagery, medical massage, meditation, tai chi, qigong, yoga, and nutrition counseling. All veterans enrolled in VA health care can request Whole Health services through their local VA facility at no additional cost.

konular.baslik: VA Benefits — Project 112/SHAD Chemical Testing Veterans
konular.ozet: Veterans who participated in Project 112 or Project SHAD, the U.S. military's chemical and biological agent testing programs conducted between 1962 and 1974, may qualify for tax-free monthly disability compensation and VA health care if they developed related health conditions. Disability claims can be filed online, by mail, in person, or with the help of a trained representative. For assistance, veterans can also contact the VA Special Issue Hotline at 800-749-8387.

konular.baslik: GI Bill — Studying at Foreign Schools
konular.ozet: Eligible veterans, service members, reservists, and qualified dependents can use VA education benefits to attend approved foreign institutions offering degrees equivalent to accredited U.S. colleges. Post-9/11 GI Bill users can receive tuition, fees, housing allowance, and a books-and-supplies stipend; MGIB-AD, MGIB-SR, and DEA recipients receive monthly payments. Before enrolling, verify the specific program is VA-approved using the GI Bill Comparison Tool — unapproved programs are not covered.

konular.baslik: GI Bill — Correspondence and Distance Learning
konular.ozet: Veterans using the Post-9/11 GI Bill, Montgomery GI Bill Active Duty, or Montgomery GI Bill Selected Reserve can apply their education benefits to approved correspondence training courses, with reimbursement for tuition and fees up to program limits or a percentage of completed lessons depending on the GI Bill chapter used. Dependents using the Fry Scholarship or Survivors' and Dependents' Educational Assistance (DEA/Chapter 35) are not eligible for correspondence training benefits. Confirm program approval through the GI Bill Comparison Tool before enrolling.

konular.baslik: VA Disability — Asbestos Exposure During Service
konular.ozet: Veterans who were exposed to asbestos during military service in roles such as mining, shipyard work, construction, carpentry, demolition, or handling insulation, roofing, or brake materials may qualify for tax-free monthly VA disability compensation for related health conditions including mesothelioma, lung cancer, and asbestosis. To receive compensation, a veteran must provide medical records, service documentation, and a physician's statement linking the diagnosed condition to asbestos contact during service. File a claim online at VA.gov or with the help of an accredited representative.

konular.baslik: IBM SkillsBuild — Free Career Courses for Veterans
konular.ozet: The IBM SkillsBuild program offers more than 1,000 free online courses to eligible veterans, active-duty service members, National Guard and Reserve members, and their spouses, children, and caregivers (age 18+) in fields including data analytics, cybersecurity, AI, and more. Courses are available as guided learning experiences with live sessions or as flexible self-paced programs, and both formats award industry-recognized credentials upon completion. Access the program and enroll through VA.gov.

konular.baslik: VA Disability — Mustard Gas or Lewisite Exposure
konular.ozet: Veterans exposed to mustard gas or Lewisite at documented locations such as Bari, Italy on December 2, 1943, or at U.S. military testing facilities during the WWII era may qualify for tax-free monthly VA disability compensation. Eligibility requires military records documenting the exposure and a diagnosed health condition believed to have been caused by contact with those agents. For veterans who served at specified locations during designated timeframes, VA presumes the exposure occurred, eliminating the need to independently prove the service connection.

konular.baslik: VA Disability — Gulf War Illness (Afghanistan Veterans)
konular.ozet: Veterans who served in Afghanistan since August 2, 1990, received an honorable discharge, and have a qualifying illness may receive tax-free monthly VA disability compensation for Gulf War illness presumptive conditions including chronic fatigue syndrome, fibromyalgia, functional gastrointestinal disorders, and certain infectious diseases such as malaria and Q fever. For presumptive conditions, veterans do not need to prove that service caused the illness — only that they meet the service requirements. File a claim at VA.gov.

konular.baslik: VA Automobile Allowance and Adaptive Equipment
konular.ozet: Veterans with service-connected disabilities affecting mobility — such as loss or permanent loss of use of one or both feet or hands, severe vision impairment, severe burns, ALS, or ankylosis of the ankle or knee — may qualify for a one-time VA automobile allowance grant to help purchase a vehicle or for adaptive equipment grants to modify a vehicle for driving. Veterans can receive either grant separately or both together, depending on their needs. File using VA Form 21-4502 (auto allowance) or VA Form 10-1394 (adaptive equipment) through VA.gov or a local VA office.

konular.baslik: VA Disability — Claims for VA-Caused Injuries (38 U.S.C. 1151)
konular.ozet: Under Title 38 U.S.C. 1151, veterans who developed a new disability or had an existing condition worsen as a direct result of VA medical care, treatment, health exams, or VA-sponsored vocational rehabilitation programs may file for additional compensation. Veterans must show that the added disability occurred because of the VA medical care or a VA employment assistance program, not as an unavoidable result of the underlying condition being treated. Claims can be filed online, by mail, in person, or with the help of an accredited representative through VA.gov.

konular.baslik: VA Prestabilization Rating (Temporary Disability on Separation)
konular.ozet: Recently separated service members with severe, unstable service-connected disabilities may receive a VA prestabilization rating of 50% or 100% — depending on the severity of the condition — providing immediate temporary disability compensation for one year after discharge while the condition stabilizes. This rating is separate from the permanent disability rating that follows and is designed to ensure veterans are not left without income during the period when their disability status is still being evaluated. Contact VA or an accredited representative to determine eligibility at the time of separation.

konular.baslik: VA Temporary 100% Rating After Surgery or Immobilization
konular.ozet: Veterans who undergo surgery or receive other treatment at a VA facility for a service-connected disability and experience severe recovery issues — such as unhealed surgical wounds, amputations, or major joint immobilization — may qualify for a temporary 100% VA disability rating lasting one to three months, with possible extension to six months for severe cases. Eligibility requires at least one month of expected recovery time or the presence of qualifying post-surgical complications. Apply online at VA.gov or call 800-827-1000 to file a claim for the temporary increase.

Veterans with service-connected disabilities who spend more than 21 days in a VA hospital or a VA-approved facility receive a temporary 100% disability rating with correspondingly higher compensation for the duration of their stay. The rating reverts to the veteran's standard service-connected rating upon discharge. File a claim for this temporary increase online at VA.gov, by mail, or with the assistance of an accredited representative.

konular.baslik: TRICARE Prime Option — US Family Health Plan (USFHP)
konular.ozet: The US Family Health Plan (USFHP) is a TRICARE Prime option offered through six regional not-for-profit health systems, providing comprehensive care — including prescriptions — through assigned primary care providers in their affiliated networks. Eligible beneficiaries include active duty family members, retirees and their families, activated National Guard and Reserve members and families, survivors, and Medal of Honor recipients living in designated service areas in the Northeast, Upper Midwest, Texas/Louisiana, and the Pacific Northwest/California. Active duty family members pay no out-of-pocket costs for authorized care.

TRICARE Prime Overseas is a managed care option available in overseas areas near military hospitals and clinics, providing care through an assigned primary care manager at military facilities with specialist referrals coordinated through International SOS. Eligible enrollees include active duty service members, command-sponsored family members, and activated National Guard and Reserve members with command-sponsored dependents; retirees are not eligible to enroll. There are no enrollment fees or copayments for care authorized through referral from a primary care manager.

TRICARE Prime Remote Overseas is a managed care option for active duty service members, command-sponsored family members, and activated National Guard and Reserve members living in designated remote overseas locations across three regions: Eurasia-Africa, Latin America and Canada, and the Pacific. Like TRICARE Prime Overseas, care is coordinated through a primary care manager and International SOS, but this plan specifically serves the most remote overseas assignments where standard Prime Overseas is not available. Retirees and their families are not eligible.

konular.baslik: DoD Safe Helpline — Sexual Assault Support
konular.ozet: The DoD Safe Helpline is the Department of Defense's sole secure, confidential, and anonymous crisis support service for members of the military community affected by sexual assault, including active-duty service members, veterans, and their loved ones. It provides 24/7 phone support at 877-995-5247, online chat, and referrals to local responders and resources tailored to the military community. The service is designed to give survivors the information and support they need while maintaining complete anonymity.

VA publishes current monthly disability compensation payment amounts for each disability percentage (10% through 100%), with separate tables for veterans with no dependents, with a spouse, and with children, adjusted annually for cost-of-living (COLA) to match Social Security benefit adjustments. Effective December 1, 2025, rates range from $180.42 at 10% to $3,938.58 at 100% for a veteran with no dependents. Check the official VA rates page for the most current figures, as amounts update each December 1.

Special Monthly Compensation (SMC) is a higher rate of VA disability compensation paid to veterans — and in some cases their spouses, surviving spouses, or parents — who have certain severe disabilities such as loss or loss of use of a limb, total blindness, being permanently bedridden, or needing daily help with basic activities like eating, dressing, and bathing. SMC is organized into tiers (K through T, plus SMC-S) with monthly rates that range from a small supplement at the K level to over $11,000 per month at the highest tier for a veteran alone. SMC is often paid in addition to standard disability compensation — veterans do not need to file a separate claim, as VA evaluates SMC eligibility when reviewing disability claims.

The Tuition Assistance Top-Up program allows active-duty service members who qualify for Montgomery GI Bill Active Duty to use GI Bill benefits to cover the difference between a course's full cost and what the Department of Defense Tuition Assistance (TA) program pays, for up to 36 months total. One month of MGIB-AD entitlement is charged for each top-up payment received, and combined DoD TA and VA payments cannot exceed the actual course cost. To apply, contact your school's certifying official and ensure you are enrolled in the MGIB-AD program.

The VA Work-Study Program pays eligible veterans and dependents who are enrolled at least three-quarter time under an approved GI Bill program to work part-time at VA offices, hospitals, educational institutions, or Veterans Service Organizations. Compensation is the federal minimum wage or the applicable state minimum wage — whichever is higher — with the opportunity to earn the institution's standard rate for some positions. Apply using VA Form 22-8691; search for open positions through VA.gov.

konular.baslik: GI Bill — National Test Reimbursement (SAT, GRE, LSAT, etc.)
konular.ozet: Eligible GI Bill recipients under Post-9/11, Montgomery GI Bill Active Duty, Montgomery GI Bill Selected Reserve, or Chapter 35 can receive reimbursement for approved national standardized test fees including college admissions tests (SAT, ACT, TOEFL), graduate school tests (GRE, GMAT, LSAT, MAT), pre-health exams (MCAT, DAT), and advanced credit tests (AP, CLEP, DSST). Veterans may submit unlimited reimbursement requests and receive the full test cost back, with a corresponding deduction from remaining GI Bill entitlement. Submit VA Form 22-0810 along with receipts and test results.

TSGLI provides short-term financial support of $25,000 to $100,000 to service members who experience qualifying traumatic injuries while covered by SGLI. Eligible members must have suffered the injury before leaving military service and experienced a scheduled loss — such as loss of a limb, sight, hearing, or the inability to perform daily activities — within two years of that injury. Benefits are paid directly to the service member or their fiduciary to help cover expenses during recovery.

The VA Foreign Medical Program provides coverage for eligible veterans receiving medical care outside the United States for service-connected conditions. Veterans can use any licensed foreign healthcare provider, and coverage extends to medically necessary services for VA-rated disabilities or conditions associated with those disabilities. To access benefits, veterans should obtain their FMP benefits letter and share it with their provider for claim submission.

The Department of Veterans Affairs provides headstones, grave markers, and medallions at no cost to honor deceased veterans, service members, and eligible family members buried in private cemeteries who meet specific service requirements. Eligibility depends on discharge status, length of service, and burial location. Applications are submitted using VA Form 40-1330 online, by mail, or by fax, along with supporting discharge documents such as the DD-214.

The VA assigns enrolled veterans to one of eight priority groups based on factors including military service history, disability rating, income level, and Medicaid eligibility. These priority groups determine how much, if anything, a veteran pays toward the cost of VA care — with veterans having service-connected disabilities receiving the highest priorities and lower cost-sharing, and higher-income veterans without disabilities receiving the lowest priority. Priority group assignment affects copay requirements and influences enrollment.

The Small Business Administration offers specialized support for veteran entrepreneurs including training programs (Boots to Business, Women Veteran Entrepreneurship Training, Service-Disabled Veteran Entrepreneurship Training), access to SBA loan programs, and federal contracting opportunities that reserve an annual portion of contracting dollars for veteran-owned businesses. Veterans Business Outreach Centers nationwide provide mentorship, business plan workshops, and guidance at no cost. Service-disabled veteran-owned businesses may separately qualify for the SDVOSB set-aside contracting program.

The VA Fiduciary Program protects veterans and beneficiaries who are unable to manage their VA benefit payments due to injury, disease, or age by appointing a fiduciary — typically a family member or a qualified professional — to oversee their finances responsibly. A field examination is conducted to assess the veteran's needs and identify an appropriate fiduciary, who is then monitored through periodic accountings and field visits. Veterans in this program continue to receive their VA benefits, which are paid to or managed by the fiduciary on their behalf.

VA copays are charges some veterans must pay for certain health care services, with amounts determined by their priority group, disability rating, and income level. Some veterans — including those with service-connected disabilities rated at 10% or higher, Medal of Honor recipients, and those below VA income thresholds — are fully exempt from copays. For veterans who do owe copays, rates vary by service type, covering medication copays, inpatient stays, and specialty visits.

A VA Supplemental Claim allows veterans and beneficiaries to request reconsideration of a past benefit decision by submitting new and relevant evidence — such as medical records not previously reviewed — or by requesting review based on a change in law such as newly recognized presumptive conditions. This is one of three decision review options under the Appeals Modernization Act, and there is no filing deadline as long as new and relevant evidence exists. Veterans can file online for disability compensation or by mail for other benefit types using VA Form 20-0995.

A VA Higher-Level Review is a reconsideration of an existing VA decision by a more senior claims adjudicator, who assesses whether errors in fact or law warrant changing the original determination. No new evidence may be submitted; the reviewer works only from the existing record and may identify duty-to-assist errors that require additional information gathering. Claims must be filed within one year of the initial decision, and an optional informal conference with the reviewer can be requested.

TRICARE covers urgent care for nonemergency conditions — such as high fevers, sprains, or minor injuries — that need attention before the situation becomes a serious health risk. Eligibility and cost-sharing depend on the specific TRICARE plan: TRICARE Prime members generally need a referral or must use a military facility, while TRICARE Select beneficiaries can use any authorized urgent care center with point-of-service fees applying for non-network providers. Using urgent care without a referral under TRICARE Prime may result in higher cost-sharing.

The Board of Veterans' Appeals is an administrative appellate body within the VA, created in 1933, that issues final decisions on veteran benefit appeals under the Appeals Modernization Act. Veterans can choose among three docket options when appealing to the Board: a direct review without new evidence or a hearing, evidence submission without a hearing, or a hearing before a Veterans Law Judge with or without new evidence. The Board is the last administrative stop before a veteran may appeal to the U.S. Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims.

konular.baslik: VA Pre-Discharge Claim — Benefits Delivery at Discharge (BDD)
konular.ozet: Service members on full-time active duty with 180 to 90 days remaining before separation can file a disability claim through the Benefits Delivery at Discharge program, which is designed to speed up VA decision-making so benefits begin as soon as possible after leaving service. Applicants must be available for VA medical examinations within 45 days of submitting the claim. Claims with fewer than 90 days until discharge can still be filed but follow a different quick-start process rather than BDD.

konular.baslik: Airborne Hazards and Open Burn Pit Registry
konular.ozet: The VA's Airborne Hazards and Open Burn Pit Registry is a health surveillance program for veterans and service members who deployed to eligible locations between 1990 and 2021 and may have been exposed to airborne hazards from open burn pits, smoke, or other toxins. Veterans deployed to qualifying locations are automatically enrolled based on Department of Defense records, though participation is voluntary and does not affect VA benefits eligibility. Enrolled veterans can receive a free exposure health exam, and the registry data supports ongoing VA research into long-term health effects.

Army Emergency Relief (AER) is a nonprofit organization established in 1942 that provides zero-interest loans and grants to Army service members — active duty, National Guard, Reserve, and retired — and their families to cover financial emergencies such as emergency travel, housing costs, medical or dental expenses, utilities, food, and natural disaster preparation. Requests are evaluated on a case-by-case basis by local installation AER offices; eligible populations also include surviving spouses, Wounded Warriors, and parents of service members. Applications and the online loan portal are available at armyemergencyrelief.org.

konular.baslik: Interstate Compact — Educational Rights for Military Children
konular.ozet: The Interstate Compact on Educational Opportunity for Military Children (MIC3) is an agreement among all 50 states, Washington D.C., and DoDEA schools that protects military children during PCS moves by standardizing school transition policies: provisional enrollment using existing records, recognition of prior coursework and advanced programs, maintained eligibility for extracurricular activities despite missed deadlines, course waivers for equivalent coursework, coordinated graduation for senior-year transfers, and comparable special education services under current IEPs. Families access Compact rights through installation school liaisons or Military OneSource at 800-342-9647; state-specific resources and a parent guide are available at mic3.net.

konular.baslik: VA Disability Claim — Process and Timeline
konular.ozet: After a veteran files a VA disability claim, the VA follows a seven-step process: claim received, initial review, evidence gathering, evidence review, rating decision, preparation of the decision letter, and final review before mailing — with an average processing time of approximately 72 days as of April 2026. The evidence-gathering phase is typically the longest; the VA may schedule a Compensation and Pension (C&P) exam, request private medical records, and review existing VA records during this phase, and submitting new evidence after it begins restarts the process. Claim status can be tracked online at VA.gov or by calling 800-827-1000.

konular.baslik: VA Disability — Benefits Unlocked at Each Rating Level
konular.ozet: VA disability ratings unlock different benefits at increasing thresholds: any service-connected rating (including 0%) makes a veteran eligible for VA health care and for VALife whole-life insurance without a medical exam; ratings of 10% or higher activate monthly tax-free disability compensation payments; 30% or higher opens additional financial support for qualifying dependents; and a 100% combined rating adds dental care, commissary and exchange privileges, and related enhanced benefits. Veterans can update their VA profile at VA.gov to report life events — such as marriage or the birth of a child — that may affect their compensation amount.

Veterans and dependents receiving education benefits under the Post-9/11 GI Bill, Montgomery GI Bill Active Duty (MGIB-AD), Montgomery GI Bill Selected Reserve (MGIB-SR), or Survivors' and Dependents' Educational Assistance (DEA/Chapter 35) must verify their enrollment — confirming credit or clock hours and enrollment dates — each month to continue receiving benefit payments. The VA sends a monthly text or email prompt; verification can be completed online at VA.gov, by text, by phone, or through Ask VA. STEM Scholarship recipients follow a separate verification process.

Veterans enrolled in VA health care and registered as patients at a VA facility can access, review, print, and download their complete VA medical records — including lab and test results, vaccine and allergy records, and care summaries and notes — through My HealtheVet on VA.gov. The Share My Health Data app allows veterans to track vitals and share that data directly with their VA care team, and CHAMPVA beneficiaries can also review their medication records through the same platform. My HealtheVet support is available Monday–Friday, 8 a.m.–8 p.m. ET; general VA.gov account questions are supported 24/7.

konular.baslik: VA Home Loan Benefit for Surviving Spouses
konular.ozet: Surviving spouses of veterans who died in service or from a service-connected disability may be eligible for VA-backed home loans — including purchase loans, Interest Rate Reduction Refinance Loans (IRRRL), and cash-out refinance loans — provided they did not remarry (or remarried after age 57 or after December 16, 2003). Spouses receiving Dependency and Indemnity Compensation (DIC) apply using VA Form 26-1817 with the veteran's DD-214; those not receiving DIC submit VA Form 21P-534EZ along with the DD-214, marriage license, and death certificate. Once eligibility is established, a Certificate of Eligibility (COE) is obtained and the loan is processed through any VA-approved private lender.

konular.baslik: VA Principles of Excellence — School Accountability
konular.ozet: The Principles of Excellence program requires schools receiving GI Bill funding to provide each enrolled veteran or dependent with a written cost estimate covering benefits, available financial aid, and projected loan debt; to assign a dedicated academic and financial advisor with disability counseling access; and to permit active-duty and Reserve members to take leave for service obligations without academic penalty. Schools must also comply with Title IV refund policies and eliminate deceptive recruitment practices; foreign schools, high schools, and tuition-free institutions are exempt. Veterans can identify participating schools and check compliance using the VA's GI Bill Comparison Tool at va.gov.

Veterans with a discharge that isn't honorable can apply for a discharge upgrade or correction; strong cases include discharges connected to mental health conditions (including PTSD), traumatic brain injury (TBI), military sexual trauma (MST), or sexual orientation under the Don't Ask, Don't Tell policy. The VA provides an online tool at va.gov/discharge-upgrade-instructions that asks questions about your situation and tells you exactly how and where to apply — it does not start the application itself. For help, call the VA at 800-827-1000, Monday through Friday, 8 a.m. to 9 p.m. ET.

The Savings Deposit Program (SDP) allows service members deployed to a designated combat zone, qualified hazardous duty area, or certain contingency operations for more than 30 consecutive days (or at least one day in each of three consecutive months) to deposit up to $10,000 of unallotted pay and earn a guaranteed 10% annual interest rate, compounded quarterly. Interest accrues for the duration of the qualifying deployment plus up to 90 days after leaving the combat zone; the account is closed and all funds are returned via direct deposit 120 days after the member's last day in the combat zone. Enrollment is initiated through your installation's finance office or unit pay administrator.

The Military Lending Act (MLA) caps the Military Annual Percentage Rate (MAPR) at 36% on most consumer loans to active-duty service members, their spouses, and certain dependents — including National Guard members mobilized federally for 30 or more consecutive days — covering credit cards, payday loans, vehicle title loans, installment loans, and overdraft lines of credit, but not residential mortgages or vehicle purchase loans secured by the vehicle. Lenders subject to the MLA cannot require mandatory arbitration agreements, force repayment through military pay allotments, or charge prepayment penalties. Violations can be reported to the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) online or by calling 855-411-2372.

konular.baslik: Air & Space Forces Aid Society (AFAS) — Emergency Financial Aid
konular.ozet: The Air & Space Forces Aid Society (AFAS) provides no-interest loans and grants to active-duty and retired Airmen, Guardians, and their families facing unexpected financial emergencies. Education grants, loans, and scholarships are available to dependent children and spouses of active-duty and retired Air Force and Space Force members. AFAS also offers community programs covering child care, vehicle upkeep, and spouse employment education; contact your installation's Military Family Readiness Center or visit afas.org to apply.

konular.baslik: Citizenship for Spouses and Children of Service Members
konular.ozet: Spouses of U.S. citizen service members stationed abroad may naturalize overseas without traveling to the United States under INA 319(e) by filing Form N-400 through the USCIS overseas office, provided they are a lawful permanent resident authorized to accompany their spouse on official orders and reside abroad in marital union; under INA 319(b), spouses present in the U.S. as LPRs may qualify for expedited naturalization with no prior residence or physical presence requirement. Children accompanying a parent on official orders abroad may complete their entire naturalization process — including the oath — overseas by filing Form N-600K under INA 322, and are not required to have lawful permanent resident status. Surviving spouses, children, or parents of U.S. citizens who die during a period of honorable active-duty service may also be eligible for naturalization.

konular.baslik: Military Spouses Residency Relief Act (MSRRA) — State Tax Protection
konular.ozet: Under the Military Spouses Residency Relief Act (MSRRA), military spouses may choose their state of legal domicile for income tax purposes from three options: the service member's domicile, the spouse's own domicile, or the state of the service member's permanent duty station. A spouse who earns wages in a state other than their chosen domicile generally does not owe income tax to that state — they owe it only to the domicile state, preventing military families from facing double state taxation as they move on PCS orders. The spouse's choice must match the service member's choice; contact your installation's legal assistance office or Military OneSource (1-800-342-9647) for guidance on updating state withholding.

konular.baslik: Overseas Cost of Living Allowance (OCONUS COLA)
konular.ozet: Overseas Cost of Living Allowance (COLA) is a non-taxable monthly allowance paid to service members stationed outside the continental United States (OCONUS) to offset higher prices for non-housing goods and services, equalizing purchasing power with members stationed in the U.S. The amount varies by pay grade, number of authorized command-sponsored dependents, and overseas location; members living on-post or shipboard typically receive reduced COLA. Rates are set by the Defense Travel Management Office (DTMO), which provides an online calculator at travel.dod.mil to estimate individual COLA entitlements.

Veterans who experienced military sexual trauma (MST) — defined by the VA as sexual assault or sexual harassment during military service — may qualify for disability compensation if it caused or worsened a condition such as PTSD, depression, or anxiety. Direct evidence (contemporaneous reports or medical records) is not required; indirect evidence showing behavioral or life changes afterward — including work performance decline, substance use, relationship problems, or medical records for related physical or mental health conditions — is accepted. MST-related VA health care is available regardless of discharge status or length of service; to file a disability claim, apply at VA.gov or contact a Veterans Service Organization (VSO) representative for free assistance.

konular.baslik: Privatized Military Housing — Tenant Bill of Rights
konular.ozet: The FY 2020 National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA) established a Tenant Bill of Rights (10 USC 2890) for residents of privatized on-installation military housing, guaranteeing housing that meets health and safety standards with functioning utilities and maintained common areas. Residents have the right to a housing advocate designated by the installation chain of command, prompt professional repairs within disclosed timeframes — with life-safety issues requiring landlord-funded relocation if unresolved within 30 days — and neutral third-party arbitration for disputes over repairs, damages, and payments. Tenants have full anti-retaliation protection; report housing concerns to your installation's Housing Office or unit housing advocate.

konular.baslik: Supportive Services for Veteran Families (SSVF) — Homelessness Prevention
konular.ozet: The Supportive Services for Veteran Families (SSVF) program provides rapid re-housing and homelessness prevention services to low-income veteran families who are homeless or at imminent risk of losing housing; services — including case management, rental assistance, and housing stability support — are delivered by VA-funded private non-profit organizations in local communities, not directly by the VA. Veterans can find nearby SSVF providers through the SSVF Provider Intake List on the VA's program page, or call the National Call Center for Homeless Veterans at 877-424-3838 (24/7, free, confidential). The program has operated since FY 2012 and is designed to be flexible and Veteran-centered, addressing individual housing needs.

konular.baslik: Career Sea Pay (CSP) — Sea Duty Incentive
konular.ozet: Active-duty enlisted members and commissioned officers assigned to sea duty earn Career Sea Pay (CSP) based on pay grade and cumulative years of sea duty, up to a maximum of $730 per month. After 36 consecutive months of sea duty, members also receive a Career Sea Pay Premium (CSP-P) of up to $350 per month for each additional consecutive month of consecutive sea duty. CSP applies to qualifying sea duty assignments across the Army, Navy, Marine Corps, Air Force, and Coast Guard; current pay tables by service branch and grade are available on DFAS's pay tables page at dfas.mil.

konular.baslik: Selective Service Registration — Requirements for Non-Citizens
konular.ozet: Most male non-citizens between ages 18 and 25 who reside in the United States — including lawful permanent residents (green card holders), refugees, and asylees — must register with the Selective Service System within 30 days of their 18th birthday; late registration is accepted until the 26th birthday. Failure to register can make a non-citizen ineligible for U.S. naturalization, federal employment, federal student financial aid, and certain other federal benefits. Active-duty service members are exempt from the registration requirement while on continuous active duty from age 18 through 26; register or verify registration status at sss.gov.

konular.baslik: Citizenship and Immigration Benefits for Surviving Military Families
konular.ozet: The surviving spouse, child, or parent of a U.S. citizen who dies during a period of honorable active-duty service may be eligible for naturalization under INA Section 319(d) without the normal continuous residence or physical presence requirements. A non-citizen service member who dies during qualifying military hostilities may be granted posthumous U.S. citizenship under INA Section 329A; once granted, surviving family members may derive additional immigration benefits from that status. Applications for posthumous citizenship must be filed within two years of the service member's death; the USCIS Family-Based Survivor Benefits page provides full details, required forms, and filing procedures for each family relationship type.

The Selective Reenlistment Bonus (SRB) is a cash incentive for enlisted service members (E-3 and above) who reenlist in military occupational specialties designated as critical for retention by their branch. The bonus is calculated as: Monthly Basic Pay × Additional Years Obligated (up to 6) × a Multiplier set by the Secretary of the relevant service, with a statutory cap of $100,000 per reenlistment contract. Each branch publishes its current SRB-eligible specialties and multipliers; check with your unit career counselor or your branch's personnel command — eligible members typically must reenlist within 90 days of their expiration of term of service (ETS).

konular.baslik: Military Power of Attorney — Types and How to Get One
konular.ozet: A military power of attorney (POA) authorizes a trusted person to act on a service member’s behalf while deployed or unavailable; types include general POA (broad authority over finances, property, and contracts), special/limited POA (specific to one transaction or account), durable POA (remains valid if the service member becomes incapacitated), and springing POA (activates only upon a defined event). POA preparation is completely free for service members through installation Legal Assistance Offices; units often schedule legal services to come to the unit before deployments. A POA typically expires at the end of its stated period or event, and the service member can revoke it at any time while mentally competent.

Special Duty Assignment Pay (SDAP) is a monthly incentive for enlisted service members (E-3 and above) certified and serving in especially demanding special duty assignments such as drill sergeant, recruiter, or embassy security guard. SDAP is structured in multiple pay levels (SD-1 through SD-6) based on the difficulty and demands of the specific position; only enlisted members on active duty or authorized inactive duty training are eligible. Current SDAP rates by duty position and level are published in the official DFAS pay tables at dfas.mil.

konular.baslik: Aviation Career Incentive Pay (ACIP) — Military Pilots
konular.ozet: Aviation Career Incentive Pay (ACIP) is a monthly incentive for commissioned officers who hold or are training for an aeronautical rating and remain in aviation careers on a career basis; monthly rates increase with years of aviation service, rising significantly at the six-year and ten-year marks, then tapering toward end of career. Enlisted aircrew receive the related Career Enlisted Flyer Incentive Pay (CEFIP) under a separate rate schedule. Current ACIP and CEFIP rates by years of aviation service and service branch are published in the DFAS pay tables at dfas.mil.

konular.baslik: Military No-Fee Passport for DoD-Ordered Travel
konular.ozet: Service members traveling on official DoD orders to countries with a Status of Forces Agreement (SOFA) may enter and exit using their military ID card and travel orders without a tourist passport; when such travel is authorized by official orders, eligible family members accompanying the service member receive a no-fee regular passport book with a special endorsement at no cost. No-fee passports are issued specifically for official government travel and are distinct from tourist (fee) passports, which service members still need for personal travel or leave. Apply through your installation’s passport office or the State Department’s Special Issuance Agency (SIA) at travel.state.gov.

Submarine Duty Incentive Pay (SUBPAY) is a monthly special pay for active-duty members who are qualified submariners assigned to submarine operations; rates scale by pay grade and years of submarine service, ranging from entry-level rates for junior enlisted to higher rates for senior enlisted with extended submarine service, and similarly for officers. Both qualified submariner designation and active assignment to submarine duty are required; members in an authorized submarine training pipeline also qualify. Current rates by grade and years of service are in the official DFAS Submarine Duty Pay tables at dfas.mil.

konular.baslik: Diving Duty Pay — Hazardous Duty Incentive
konular.ozet: Diving Duty Pay is a Hazardous Duty Incentive Pay (37 U.S.C. 301) for military members actively performing diving duties or enrolled in an authorized diving training pipeline; rates vary by pay grade, branch, and whether the billet is standard diving or special operations. Pay is billet-dependent — completing dive school alone does not entitle a member to dive pay without assignment to a coded diving duty position. Current official rates by branch and pay grade are in the DFAS Dive Pay tables at dfas.mil.

konular.baslik: DoD Adoption Reimbursement for Military Families
konular.ozet: Active-duty, National Guard, and reserve service members can receive DoD reimbursement for qualifying adoption expenses up to $2,000 per child, with a maximum of $5,000 per calendar year; adopted children must be enrolled in DEERS within 90 days of adoption to receive TRICARE coverage. Service members may also be eligible for 12 weeks of non-chargeable paid parental leave after adoption, and families may qualify for a federal adoption tax credit of approximately $15,950 per child. Free, confidential adoption consultations are available through Military OneSource at 800-342-9647.

konular.baslik: VA National Veterans Sports Programs — Adaptive Sports
konular.ozet: The VA National Veterans Sports Programs award approximately $16 million in annual grants to more than 90 national, regional, and community programs providing adaptive sports and recreational activities for veterans and service members with disabilities, serving more than 15,000 participants per year. Activities include Paralympic sports, archery, cycling, skiing, equine therapy, rock climbing, and other activities customized to support rehabilitation, independence, and quality of life. To find adaptive sports programs near you, visit department.va.gov/veteran-sports or contact your nearest VA medical center's Recreation Therapy department.

konular.baslik: Military Leave — Types, Accrual, and Rules
konular.ozet: Service members earn 30 days of paid annual leave per year (2.5 days per month), with a standard carryover maximum of 60 days at the end of each fiscal year (September 30); emergency leave is approved quickly and charged against the leave balance, while convalescent leave (after medical conditions or childbirth) is non-chargeable and typically up to 30 days. Non-chargeable parental leave of 12 weeks is available within one year of a qualifying birth, adoption, or foster care event; bereavement leave of up to 14 consecutive days is also non-chargeable. Terminal leave is regular chargeable leave taken immediately before separation; service members may sell back up to a career maximum of 60 days of unused leave.

konular.baslik: Medical Evaluation Board (MEB) and Disability Separation
konular.ozet: The Medical Evaluation Board (MEB) is initiated when a service member's physician believes the member cannot return to duty or has reached a medical retention decision point; the board examines the medical history, documents the injury or illness, and determines whether the member meets military medical retention standards — possible outcomes are returning to duty, continued service in a different specialty, or referral to the Physical Evaluation Board (PEB). The PEB formally determines fitness for continued service and establishes a disability rating using the VA Schedule for Rating Disabilities (VASRD); members rated 30% or higher are placed on the Temporary or Permanent Disability Retired List, while those rated below 20% typically receive disability severance pay. Entering the MEB does not automatically result in discharge; complete medical documentation is essential and service members should consult a Judge Advocate (JAG officer) throughout the process.

konular.baslik: Basic Needs Allowance (BNA) — Low-Income Military Families
konular.ozet: The Basic Needs Allowance (BNA) is a monthly tax-free allowance for active-duty service members — including activated National Guard members and reservists — who have at least one dependent registered in DEERS and whose gross household income falls below 200% of the federal poverty guidelines (the FY2025 NDAA raised eligibility from the original 130% threshold). Eligibility thresholds vary by household size and geographic location, and service members must reapply annually to confirm continued eligibility. Check current eligibility thresholds and apply through the official DFAS Basic Needs Allowance page at dfas.mil.

konular.baslik: USCIS Military Help Line — Immigration Support for Service Members
konular.ozet: USCIS operates a dedicated Military Help Line at 877-CIS-4MIL (877-247-4645), with email at militaryinfo@uscis.dhs.gov, available Monday through Friday 8 a.m. to 4 p.m. Central time; many installations also have a designated USCIS liaison to assist with naturalization applications. Service members stationed overseas can receive naturalization services at quarterly hub visits to Camp Humphreys (South Korea), Yokosuka (Japan), Stuttgart (Germany), and Naples (Italy); domestic processing of all overseas military Form N-400 applications is handled by the USCIS Guam Field Office (Asia/Pacific) and the Washington Field Office (Europe, Middle East, and Africa). Use the Deployments and Moving page at uscis.gov/military for guidance when military orders affect pending immigration applications.

konular.baslik: Child Custody Protections for Military Parents
konular.ozet: Under the SCRA, a deployed service member can request an automatic 90-day stay of custody proceedings if military duties prevent participation, with any additional delay at the court's discretion; all 50 states have custody provisions protecting military parents, including prohibitions on using military absence alone as grounds to modify existing custody orders and requirements that original arrangements be reinstated upon return. Service members are required to maintain an official Family Care Plan before deploying, specifying who provides financial, medical, and logistical care for children. Contact your installation's Legal Assistance Office for help preparing a family care plan and understanding your state's specific military custody protections.

The Foreign Language Proficiency Bonus (FLPB) is paid to active-duty and reserve service members who are certified as proficient in one or more languages or dialects on the DoD Strategic Language List; monthly rates range from $100 to $500 for a single language and up to $1,000 for two or more languages, with a cap of $12,000 per annual certification period. Proficiency must be certified annually using a combination of at least two of three modalities (listening, reading, speaking) at levels determined by the service branch. Current FLPB pay tables by language category and branch are available at dfas.mil.

konular.baslik: Military Estate Planning — Wills, POA, and Survivor Documents
konular.ozet: An estate plan for military families typically includes a last will and testament (distributes property and designates guardians for children), power of attorney (authorizes someone to act on your behalf), living will (specifies medical treatment preferences in an emergency), and beneficiary designations for life insurance and survivor benefits such as SGLI and SBP. Installation Legal Assistance / JAG offices draft wills and estate documents at no cost to service members; update your plan whenever you marry, divorce, change state of residence, have a child, or experience significant changes in property or family circumstances. Contact your nearest legal assistance office or call Military OneSource at 800-342-9647 to locate free legal services.

konular.baslik: Healthcare for Re-entry Veterans (HCRV) — Prison Reentry Support
konular.ozet: The VA Healthcare for Re-entry Veterans (HCRV) program places specialists in state and federal prisons nationwide to provide outreach and pre-release assessments for incarcerated veterans, helping them plan access to VA services and preventing homelessness upon release. After release, HCRV specialists provide referrals and linkages to medical, mental health, and social services including employment assistance, along with short-term case management. Veterans approaching release from prison or recently released can reach their regional HCRV specialist or call the National Call Center for Homeless Veterans at 877-424-3838 (24/7, free and confidential).

Hardship Duty Pay for Location (HDP-L) is additional monthly compensation for service members assigned to locations where living conditions are substantially below those in the continental United States; rates are $50, $100, or $150 per month for officers and enlisted in DoD-designated areas, based on severity of hardship. Entitlement begins the day of arrival at the duty location and ends the day of departure; members must serve more than 30 days at a designated location, and pay is prorated daily. Current lists of designated HDP-L locations and applicable rates are in the official DFAS pay tables at dfas.mil.

konular.baslik: American Red Cross Hero Care Network — Military Emergency Support
konular.ozet: The American Red Cross Hero Care Network provides free, confidential 24/7 emergency support to military members and their families, including verified emergency communications to service members worldwide in cases of serious illness, death, or birth of an immediate family member. The Red Cross also facilitates access to financial assistance for emergency travel, food, and shelter through military aid society partnerships, and offers resiliency workshops, a Caregiver Network for families of wounded service members, and disaster assistance. Contact the Hero Care Network at 877-272-7337 (24/7), text 'GETHEROCARE' to 90999, or create an account at saf.redcross.org.

konular.baslik: National Guard and Reserve Activation — Benefits Upon Call-Up
konular.ozet: When National Guard or Reserve members are called to active duty, their benefits change significantly: active-duty pay replaces drill pay, and members on orders of more than 30 consecutive days receive comprehensive medical and dental care at no cost, with families also covered by TRICARE. For contingency operations, active-duty TRICARE may begin up to 180 days before the reporting date from the date orders are issued; after deactivation from a contingency operation, members and their families are covered by the Transitional Assistance Management Program (TAMP) for 180 days. Contact Military OneSource at 800-342-9647 or your unit readiness officer for details on benefits as orders are issued.

Under the Combat Zone Tax Exclusion, U.S. Armed Forces members who serve in a presidentially designated combat zone may exclude qualifying military compensation from federal income tax. Excluded pay includes basic pay for each month present in the combat zone, reenlistment bonuses executed while deployed, imminent danger/hostile fire pay, and income from selling leave accrued in the zone. The exclusion is applied automatically and reflected on the member's W-2.

konular.baslik: Citizenship for Children Born Abroad to Service Members
konular.ozet: Children born outside the United States to a U.S. citizen parent who is a member of the Armed Forces may acquire citizenship at birth if the parent meets applicable physical presence or residence requirements; periods of honorable military service count toward those requirements. Children who do not acquire citizenship at birth may be eligible under INA Section 320 (automatic acquisition) or INA Section 322 (naturalization through a parent), and those residing abroad with a parent on official orders may complete the entire naturalization process outside the United States. Eligible children may document their citizenship by obtaining a Consular Report of Birth Abroad (CRBA) or by filing Form N-600.

The DoD Homeowners Assistance Program provides financial aid to eligible military homeowners who suffer losses on the sale of their primary residence due to base realignment, closure, or significant operational reductions at a nearby installation. Current eligibility includes service members with a 30% or greater service-connected disability incurred after September 11, 2001, and surviving spouses of service members who died from service-connected causes after that date. The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (USACE), Savannah District, processes all applications.

konular.baslik: GI Bill Comparison Tool — School & Program Research
konular.ozet: The VA's GI Bill Comparison Tool allows veterans, service members, and their families to research and compare GI Bill benefits at approved schools, employers, and training programs. Users can view data on tuition, graduation rates, loan default rates, Yellow Ribbon participation, accreditation status, and veteran-specific indicators such as Principles of Excellence compliance. The tool is designed to help eligible individuals make informed educational decisions before committing GI Bill benefits.

konular.baslik: Public Service Loan Forgiveness (PSLF) for Military Service
konular.ozet: Public Service Loan Forgiveness cancels the remaining balance on eligible federal Direct Loans after a borrower makes 120 qualifying monthly payments while employed full-time by a qualifying public service employer, including the U.S. military. A key provision for service members is that months spent on active duty — even during deferment or forbearance — may count toward the 120-payment requirement. Borrowers manage and apply through the PSLF Help Tool at StudentAid.gov.

The Health Professions Loan Repayment Program is a DoD incentive that repays qualifying educational loans for eligible health professionals who serve as commissioned officers in the military. The program is intended to attract new accessions and retain active-duty medical personnel in critical shortage specialties, including medicine, dentistry, osteopathic medicine, and nursing. Individual service branches publish annual lists of qualifying specialties and funding levels.
konular.kaynak_adi: U.S. Navy Bureau of Medicine and Surgery

konular.baslik: Voluntary Separation Incentive & Special Separation Benefit (VSI/SSB)
konular.ozet: The Voluntary Separation Incentive (VSI) and Special Separation Benefit (SSB) were programs that provided financial incentives to service members who voluntarily separated during congressionally authorized drawdown periods — VSI paid an annual annuity, while SSB paid a one-time lump sum at separation. Both programs have recoupment provisions: if a recipient later qualifies for VA disability compensation, the VSI or SSB amount received is offset from disability payments until fully recovered. Veterans who received these payments should contact DFAS to understand the impact on their VA compensation.

konular.baslik: Special Immigrant Visa (SIV) for Iraqi & Afghan Interpreters
konular.ozet: The Special Immigrant Visa program provides a pathway to U.S. immigrant status for Iraqi and Afghan nationals who served directly alongside U.S. Armed Forces or under Chief of Mission authority for a minimum of 12 months and received a favorable recommendation from a commanding general or flag officer. The program is authorized under separate statutory frameworks for Iraq and Afghanistan, with a limited annual allocation of visas. Applications are processed through the U.S. Department of State, with USCIS handling the petition stage via Form I-360.

Federal law — including amendments to the Servicemembers Civil Relief Act — provides military spouses with protections and reimbursements when transferring professional licenses across state lines during a Permanent Change of Station (PCS) move. Interstate occupational licensure compacts enable seamless license portability across member states for professions such as nursing, physical therapy, and counseling. Service branches may reimburse spouses for associated relicensing costs; details are available through Military OneSource.

konular.baslik: HIRE Vets Medallion Program — Veteran-Friendly Employer Recognition
konular.ozet: The HIRE Vets Medallion Program is the only federal-level veterans employment award, established under the HIRE Vets Act of 2017, which recognizes employers of all sizes for efforts to recruit, employ, and retain military veterans. Awards are offered at Gold and Platinum tiers across three employer size categories: small (50 or fewer employees), medium (51-499), and large (500 or more). Veterans can use medallion recipients as a guide when evaluating potential employers; applications open annually on January 31.

konular.baslik: Social Security Credits for Military Service
konular.ozet: Active-duty military service has been covered under Social Security since 1957. From 1957 through 2001, eligible service members received special extra earnings credits to account for untaxed military benefits — up to $1,200 per year in additional credits from 1978 through 2001. These extra credits ended in January 2002, and a military pension does not reduce Social Security retirement or disability benefits.

konular.baslik: Discharge Character & Immigration Consequences for Noncitizens
konular.ozet: Noncitizen service members must have served and separated under honorable conditions to be eligible for naturalization under INA Sections 328 or 329. A less-than-honorable discharge disqualifies a noncitizen from military naturalization; as of 2025 USCIS guidance, uncharacterized discharges issued on or after August 1, 2024 no longer meet the honorable conditions standard. Green card holders with a less-than-honorable discharge may also become subject to removal proceedings under broader immigration law.

konular.baslik: Immigrant & Nonimmigrant Visa Options for Foreign National Military Spouses
konular.ozet: Foreign national spouses of U.S. citizen service members can apply for an IR-1 (Immediate Relative) immigrant visa if married two or more years, or a CR-1 (Conditional Resident) visa if married under two years; both grant lawful permanent resident status upon entry. The K-3 nonimmigrant visa allows a spouse to enter the United States while an immigrant petition (Form I-130) is pending. These pathways are separate from Military Parole in Place, which is a discretionary option for qualifying spouses already present in the United States.

The military services offer enlistment bonuses to recruits who enlist in specific military occupational specialties or ratings deemed critical by the branch. Eligibility depends on job specialty, ASVAB scores, enlistment contract length, and training ship dates. Initial bonus payments are typically made after completion of initial entry training; bonus amounts and eligible MOSs change regularly based on recruiting needs.

Under 37 U.S. Code § 355, the Secretary of Defense may pay a retention bonus to active-duty or reserve-component members who are qualified in a critical military skill designated as a shortage specialty, or who accept assignment to a high-priority unit. Eligible members must execute a written agreement to continue serving for at least one additional year. Total payments are capped by statute, with higher limits for health care officers.

konular.baslik: VR&E Subsistence Allowance — Monthly Living Stipend
konular.ozet: Veterans participating in the VA Vocational Rehabilitation and Employment (VR&E, Chapter 31) program may receive a monthly subsistence allowance while pursuing an educational or vocational training program. The monthly amount is determined by attendance rate (full-time, three-quarter, or half-time), number of dependents, and type of training program. Veterans also eligible for the Post-9/11 GI Bill may instead receive a BAH-equivalent housing allowance as their subsistence benefit.

Under the Blended Retirement System (BRS), eligible retirees may choose to receive 25% or 50% of their pension as a lump sum at retirement in exchange for a proportionally reduced monthly payment until age 67. At age 67, the monthly retired pay automatically returns to the full, unreduced amount. The lump sum election must be made during the retirement application process and cannot be changed after separation.

konular.baslik: State Income Tax Protections for Active Duty Service Members
konular.ozet: Under the Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (SCRA), active-duty service members may retain their legal state of domicile for income tax purposes regardless of where they are stationed, so military pay is taxed only by their home state, not by the state where they are physically assigned. The Veterans Auto and Education Improvement Act of 2022 further allows service members and spouses to elect filing based on the service member's domicile, the spouse's domicile, or the permanent duty station state. SCRA protections apply only to the service member's military income; income earned from other sources remains taxable where earned.

konular.baslik: ROTC Scholarships — Army, Navy, and Air Force
konular.ozet: The Army, Navy, and Air Force offer Reserve Officers' Training Corps (ROTC) scholarship programs at participating colleges, covering tuition, fees, or room and board along with monthly stipends and book allowances for students who commit to serving as commissioned officers upon graduation. Army ROTC is available at over 1,000 colleges; Navy ROTC scholarships lead to commissions as Navy or Marine Corps officers; Air Force ROTC graduates may commission into the Air Force or Space Force. Recipients typically incur an eight-year service obligation (combination of active duty and reserve), with specifics varying by branch and scholarship type.

konular.baslik: VA Survivors Pension vs. Dependency and Indemnity Compensation (DIC)
konular.ozet: VA Survivors Pension is a needs-based monthly benefit for surviving spouses and children of wartime veterans, with eligibility based on the survivor's income and net worth. VA Dependency and Indemnity Compensation (DIC) is a service-connection-based, tax-free monthly benefit paid to survivors of service members who died in the line of duty or veterans whose death resulted from a service-connected condition, with no income or net worth requirement. Survivors who may qualify for both should apply for both; VA will pay the higher benefit.

konular.baslik: Commissary and Exchange Privileges for Veterans
konular.ozet: Certain veterans may access commissaries, military exchanges (PX/BX), and MWR facilities by presenting valid identification. Accepted IDs include the Veterans Health Identification Card (VHIC) for VA-enrolled veterans, or a DoD-issued ID card for retirees and those with a 100% VA disability rating. Medal of Honor recipients receive lifetime exchange and commissary access.

konular.baslik: SBP-DIC Offset Repeal — Widow's Tax Elimination (2023)
konular.ozet: Prior to 2023, surviving spouses receiving both a Survivor Benefit Plan (SBP) annuity and VA Dependency and Indemnity Compensation (DIC) had their SBP payment reduced dollar-for-dollar by the DIC amount. Effective February 1, 2023, this offset was fully eliminated; surviving spouses now receive their complete SBP payment from DFAS and their complete DIC payment from the VA with no reduction. The Special Survivor Indemnity Allowance (SSIA), which had partially compensated for the offset, was also discontinued after January 3, 2023.

Incarcerated veterans may retain access to certain VA benefits depending on the nature of their conviction. Disability compensation payments are reduced for felony convictions resulting in imprisonment beyond 60 days, while education benefits for felony-convicted veterans in prison are generally limited to direct costs such as tuition, fees, and books. The VA's Health Care for Re-entry Veterans (HCRV) and Veteran Justice Outreach (VJO) programs provide outreach and reintegration support to help veterans transition back into the community upon release.

konular.baslik: Veteran-Directed Care (VDC) — Self-Managed Home Care Budget
konular.ozet: Veteran-Directed Care is a VA program that allows enrolled veterans who need personal care services — such as help with bathing, dressing, or meal preparation — to manage their own allocated budget rather than receive traditional VA-assigned care. Veterans work with a counselor to hire their own workers, which may include family members, to meet daily needs at home or in the community. Eligible veterans must be enrolled in VA health care, qualify for community care, and meet the clinical criteria; availability depends on local VA participation.

Military chaplains provide confidential counseling, spiritual care, and support to service members and their families on issues including combat stress, deployment, grief, marriage difficulties, and substance abuse. They are available to all service members regardless of religious affiliation and can make referrals to clergy of specific faith traditions or licensed mental health professionals when needed. Chaplain conversations are protected by privileged communication, making them a confidential resource distinct from other military counseling.

konular.baslik: VA Home Loan — Step-by-Step Purchase Process
konular.ozet: The VA home loan purchase process begins with obtaining a Certificate of Eligibility (COE), which confirms to a lender that the buyer meets VA loan requirements. Veterans then find a VA-approved lender, get pre-qualified, work with a real estate agent to secure a purchase agreement containing a VA Option Clause, complete a loan application, and allow the lender to order a VA appraisal. The process closes through a title company or attorney once all credit, income, and appraisal documentation are reviewed and approved.

The UCX program provides unemployment benefits for eligible ex-military personnel who served on active duty and were separated under honorable conditions; benefits are administered by individual states and amounts, duration, and eligibility requirements vary by state. Benefits are funded directly by the military branches with no payroll deductions taken from servicemembers' wages during service. Separated servicemembers should file a UCX claim through their state's unemployment insurance agency.
konular.kaynak_adi: U.S. Department of Labor — Office of Unemployment Insurance

konular.ozet: Service members who perform parachute jumping as an essential military duty receive a monthly hazard pay in addition to their base pay; those performing military free fall operations (parachute deployment without a static line) receive a higher rate. Reserve component members receive a prorated daily amount for equivalent duty periods, and a member may not receive more than two simultaneous hazard pay compensations.

konular.baslik: CONUS Cost of Living Allowance (CONUS COLA)
konular.ozet: Service members assigned to duty stations within the continental United States where the local cost of living significantly exceeds the national average may be eligible for CONUS COLA. The allowance supplements base pay to help offset higher local living expenses, and current eligible locations and rates are published annually by the Defense Travel Management Office.

konular.baslik: VA Fertility and Reproductive Health Benefits
konular.ozet: The VA provides fertility counseling and certain fertility treatments, including in vitro fertilization (IVF), to veterans who have a service-connected condition that causes infertility. Eligible veterans and their spouses or partners may access covered services at VA medical centers or through authorized community providers. Contact your VA health care team or the VA health benefits hotline at 877-222-8387 to determine eligibility and available services.

konular.baslik: VA Community Living Centers (VA Nursing Homes)
konular.ozet: VA Community Living Centers (CLCs) are VA-operated nursing home facilities at over 100 sites nationwide, providing 24-hour skilled nursing care, restorative services, mental health support, dementia care, respite care, and hospice to eligible veterans. Veterans must be enrolled in VA health care, be medically stable, and have a clinical need for nursing home-level care; copay amounts depend on service-connected status and income.
konular.kaynak_adi: U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs — Geriatrics and Extended Care

konular.baslik: Special Compensation for Assistance with Activities of Daily Living (SCAADL)
konular.ozet: SCAADL is monthly special compensation for active-duty servicemembers with a permanent, severe, service-connected disability that requires personal or mechanical assistance to leave home or bed, or constant supervision to avoid physical harm. Eligibility ends 90 days after separation or retirement, upon the member's death, or when the member begins receiving equivalent VA compensation; contact your military treatment facility's SCAADL coordinator to apply.

Reserve and National Guard members become eligible for retirement pay starting at age 60 — reduced to as low as age 50 for qualifying active duty service after January 28, 2008 — after earning at least 20 qualifying years computed by retirement points. Points accumulate through active service, unit drills, and an annual membership credit, with a minimum number of points required per year for that year to count toward retirement.

Veterans Treatment Courts (VTCs) are specialized criminal justice diversion programs that pair judicial supervision with VA health care services, including mental health and substance use treatment, for justice-involved veterans. VA Veterans Justice Outreach (VJO) specialists at VA medical centers nationwide work directly with courts and law enforcement to connect veterans to VA services before or during incarceration. Veterans or family members can contact the nearest VA medical center's VJO specialist or call the National Call Center for Homeless Veterans at 877-424-3838.
konular.kaynak_adi: U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs — Veterans Justice Programs

konular.ozet: Enlisted members of the armed forces receive an annual clothing replacement allowance to help maintain required uniforms, paid as a cash allowance by DFAS. The initial clothing allowance is provided upon entry into service; annual replacement amounts and current rates are published by DFAS and differ between branches. Officers receive a one-time uniform allowance upon commissioning rather than annual replacements.

konular.baslik: DEERS Enrollment — ID Cards and Benefits Access
konular.ozet: The Defense Enrollment Eligibility Reporting System (DEERS) is the DoD database that establishes eligibility for TRICARE, military ID cards, and other benefits; servicemembers are registered automatically, but sponsors must manually enroll eligible dependents (spouse, children) at a local RAPIDS ID card office, by phone at 800-538-9552, or by mail. Keeping DEERS records current is essential — errors cause problems with TRICARE claims, referrals, and prescription coverage.

Continuation Pay is a mid-career cash bonus available to service members under the Blended Retirement System who have between 7 and 12 years of service and agree to serve at least three additional years. Active duty members receive a minimum of 2.5 times monthly basic pay, while reserve component members receive at least 0.5 times monthly basic pay, with exact amounts varying by service branch. The bonus may be deposited directly into the Thrift Savings Plan retirement account.

Veterans Mortgage Life Insurance (VMLI) provides up to $200,000 in mortgage protection insurance for veterans with severe service-connected disabilities who have received a Specially Adapted Housing (SAH) grant. Payments are made directly to the lender rather than to beneficiaries, and coverage decreases as the mortgage balance declines. Applicants must be under age 70, hold the title to an adapted home, and carry an active mortgage.

The Defense Activity for Non-Traditional Education Support (DANTES) fully funds College-Level Examination Program (CLEP) exams for eligible service members, including active duty, reserve, National Guard, and certain military spouses, allowing them to earn college credit by demonstrating existing knowledge. DANTES funding covers each exam subject once, and exam and administration fees are waived at DANTES-funded and on-base test centers. Eligible individuals register through the My CLEP student portal and must present a Geneva Convention Identification Card on test day.

Newborns must be registered in the Defense Enrollment Eligibility Reporting System (DEERS) and enrolled in a TRICARE health plan within 90 days of birth for stateside families, or within 120 days for initial DEERS registration followed by a 90-day enrollment window for overseas families. Birth and adoption are qualifying life events that trigger TRICARE eligibility for the child. Failure to register and enroll within the required timeframe may result in a gap in coverage.

Under 10 U.S.C. § 1034, service members are protected from retaliation for communicating with Members of Congress or Inspectors General regarding violations of law, gross mismanagement, waste of funds, abuse of authority, or threats to public safety. Commanders are prohibited from taking adverse personnel actions — such as unfavorable evaluations or duty reassignments — against members who make protected disclosures. Complaints are submitted to an Inspector General, who investigates outside the chain of command and must report findings within 30 days.

Morale, Welfare and Recreation (MWR) programs support the physical and mental well-being of service members and their families through installation-based fitness centers, aquatics, libraries, child development services, outdoor recreation, and recreational lodging. Programs are organized into three funding categories — mission-sustaining, community support, and revenue-generating — ensuring a broad range of services across installations. MWR also provides access to American Forces Travel discounts and the Information, Tickets and Travel (ITT) program for affordable recreation and travel opportunities.

The Integrated Disability Evaluation System (IDES) is a joint VA and Department of Defense program that streamlines the disability evaluation process for service members who are wounded, ill, or injured and cannot perform their military duties. The VA and DoD collaborate to assess medical fitness for continued service and provide a proposed disability rating to the service member before discharge. Those found medically unfit receive their VA disability determination prior to separation and gain early access to Veteran Readiness and Employment services.

konular.baslik: Board for Correction of Military Records (BCMR)
konular.ozet: The Board for Correction of Military Records (BCMR), authorized under 10 U.S.C. § 1552, is a civilian board empowered to correct errors or remove injustices from military records of current and former service members, including discharge characterizations, promotion decisions, and service-related benefits. Applications are submitted using DD Form 149 and must generally be filed within three years of discovering the error or injustice, though the board may waive this deadline in the interest of justice. Each service branch maintains its own board: the Army Review Boards Agency (ARBA), the Board for Correction of Naval Records (BCNR), and the Air Force Board for Correction of Military Records (AFBCMR).

konular.baslik: Lay and Buddy Statements for VA Claims
konular.ozet: Lay evidence and buddy statements are written testimonies submitted by veterans, family members, fellow service members, or any person with personal knowledge of a veteran's condition or service-related events, requiring no special training or credentials. These statements can be submitted on plain paper, via VA Form 21-10210 (Lay/Witness Statement), or VA Form 21-4138, and are accepted for original, increased-disability, secondary service-connection, and PTSD claims. Lay statements are particularly valuable when medical documentation is unavailable, providing firsthand accounts of service-related events or symptom progression.

When a DoD security clearance is denied or revoked, the case is adjudicated by the Defense Office of Hearings and Appeals (DOHA), whose Administrative Judges and Appeal Board apply a fair and impartial standard weighing conduct seriousness, frequency, recency, applicant age, and evidence of rehabilitation. Applicants must cooperate fully — provide truthful answers and authorize disclosure of relevant information — or DOHA may revoke the clearance and bar reapplication for one year.
