Emergency suspension authority also allows the ASD(C3I) to suspend a clearance immediately when continued access poses an imminent threat to the national interest.

konular.baslik: Political Activity Restrictions for Active-Duty Military
konular.ozet: Under 10 USC § 973, active-duty officers are prohibited from holding elective federal, state, or local civil offices, from accepting positions requiring Senate confirmation, and from taking employment that would interfere with military duties. Exceptions permit officers to hold federal civil offices when officially assigned or detailed to those positions, and to seek and hold nonpartisan civil office on an independent school board located exclusively on a military reservation. Similar restrictions apply to enlisted members and all active-duty personnel under DoD Directive 1344.10.

Under the Freedom of Information Act (5 USC § 552), any person may submit a written request to any federal agency — including DoD components — for agency records; the agency must respond within 20 business days and may charge search, duplication, and review fees depending on the requester category. Requesters whose request is denied have the right to appeal to the agency head within at least 90 days, and dispute resolution services are available through the Office of Government Information Services. FOIA.gov, managed by the Department of Justice, provides a central portal to identify the correct DoD component FOIA office and submit requests electronically.

Under 37 USC § 403(b)(6), a service member's Basic Allowance for Housing may not be reduced as long as the member retains uninterrupted eligibility within the same military housing area — protecting members from decreases caused by falling local housing costs or by the member's own promotion. This hold-harmless protection applies to domestic duty stations; a similar provision under § 403(c)(2) covers overseas locations, though those amounts may still adjust for currency fluctuations. The protection ends if eligibility is interrupted, such as when a member receives permanent change of station orders to a new location.

Under 10 USC § 710, the Secretary of each military department may place active-duty service members in an inactive-duty status for up to three years to address personal or professional needs, after which the member returns to active service. During the intermission, members receive two-thirtieths of their normal monthly basic pay and must serve in the Ready Reserve, completing training to maintain proficiency; they also retain access to military medical and dental care, survivor death benefits, and VA benefits. Service time during inactive status does not count toward retirement, and members are ineligible for promotion while in the program, with a return-to-active-duty obligation of one month for each month of inactive status.

konular.baslik: School Liaison Officers (SLO) for Military Children
konular.ozet: School Liaison Officers are located at each military installation and serve as the primary point of contact for military families, local school systems, and installation leadership on school-related matters for children in pre-K through 12th grade. They help families navigating PCS moves by providing information on school districts, assisting with credit transfers, offering youth sponsorship referrals, and explaining educational options including private, charter, parochial, and homeschool alternatives. Additional services include special education referrals through the Exceptional Family Member Program, support for deployment-related challenges, and college and career readiness guidance.

Every VA medical facility has a designated Patient Advocate — a staff member who manages feedback from veterans and their families, facilitates problem resolution, and handles concerns about care delivery in coordination with VA management and clinical staff. Veterans are encouraged to first address concerns with their treatment team or the provider's supervisor, and may escalate unresolved issues to the Patient Advocate to file appeals for care reviews and address questions about patient rights and responsibilities. Patient Advocates are available at individual VA medical centers; veterans can find the nearest facility using the VA's location finder at va.gov.

Veterans enrolled in VA health care who have received VA or in-network provider care within the past 24 months may use VA-covered walk-in urgent care at in-network community urgent care centers and retail health clinics for minor injuries and illnesses — such as strep throat, pink eye, sprained muscles, and skin or ear infections — with no referral required. Diagnostic services including X-rays and lab tests, as well as certain medicines and vaccines, are also covered; veterans should bring a valid government-issued photo ID and confirm the provider is in-network before receiving treatment. Copayments of up to $30 may apply in some cases depending on the veteran's priority group and annual visit frequency, and the VA will bill the veteran after the visit if applicable.

konular.baslik: Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims (CAVC)
konular.ozet: The U.S. Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims (CAVC) is an independent federal Article I court located in Washington, D.C., with exclusive jurisdiction under 38 USC § 7252 to review final decisions of the Board of Veterans' Appeals; the Court may affirm, modify, reverse, or remand Board decisions. Veterans who disagree with a Board decision must file a Notice of Appeal with the CAVC within 120 days of the date the Board decision notice is issued, by delivering or mailing the notice to the Court. The CAVC provides access to a searchable decisions database, a practitioner locator, electronic case filing, and public oral arguments via YouTube Live.
konular.kaynak_adi: U.S. Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims

konular.ozet: VA provides comprehensive TBI care through its Polytrauma System of Care, a national network of specialized rehabilitation programs serving veterans and service members with TBI ranging from mild to severe. Treatment includes cognitive, physical, speech, and occupational therapy, medication management, assistive devices, and a residential rehabilitation program for those requiring intensive neurobehavioral care in a supervised environment. Veterans can access TBI services by contacting their primary care provider or local VA health facility; those not yet enrolled in VA health care can apply at va.gov.

The Family and Medical Leave Act (FMLA) provides two special protections for military families employed by covered employers: qualifying exigency leave (up to 12 workweeks per year) when a spouse, child, or parent is called to covered active duty, and military caregiver leave (up to 26 workweeks in a single 12-month period) to care for a covered servicemember or veteran with a serious injury or illness. Both provisions apply to employees who have worked at least 12 months and 1,250 hours for an employer with 50 or more employees. Use DOL Forms WH-384 (exigency), WH-385 (servicemember caregiver), or WH-385-V (veteran caregiver) to document the request.
konular.kaynak_adi: U.S. Department of Labor, Wage and Hour Division

konular.ozet: The SBA administers the Veteran Small Business Certification (VetCert) program, which certifies service-disabled veteran-owned small businesses (SDVOSBs) and veteran-owned small businesses (VOSBs) to compete for federal set-aside and sole-source contracts; the federal government sets a goal of awarding at least 5% of all federal contracting dollars to SDVOSBs each year. To qualify, the business must be at least 51% owned and controlled by veterans or service-disabled veterans and meet SBA small business size standards for its industry. Veterans apply and manage their certification through the MySBA Certifications portal at certifications.sba.gov.

VA provides comprehensive hearing health care — including diagnostic evaluations, hearing aids, cochlear implants, tinnitus management, and teleaudiology — to all enrolled veterans; audiology is one of the few VA services that veterans can schedule directly without a referral from a primary care provider. Hearing aids, repairs, batteries, and accessories are provided at no cost to eligible veterans, and surgically implantable devices such as cochlear implants are available at more than 125 VA sites for those with severe to profound hearing loss. Veterans should contact their nearest VA medical center's audiology department to schedule an evaluation.

VA Solid Start proactively contacts newly separated service members by phone at approximately 90, 180, and 365 days after separation to connect them with VA benefits and support during the critical first year of transition to civilian life. Representatives help veterans access disability compensation, mental health care, VA health care enrollment, and education and employment resources tailored to each individual's situation, and follow up with a personalized email after each call. Veterans can contact VA Solid Start at 1-800-827-0611, Monday–Friday, 8 a.m.–9 p.m. ET.

Army Survivor Outreach Services (SOS) provides long-term support to surviving family members of fallen Soldiers from all components — Active, Guard, and Reserve — regardless of the Soldier's branch, duty status, location, or manner of death. Services include grief counseling, financial guidance and estate planning education, benefits coordination, and peer support groups, available for as long as the survivor desires. Survivors are honored with Gold Star and Next of Kin lapel buttons; reach a Gold Star representative toll-free at (833) 313-1960.

Assignment Incentive Pay (AIP) compensates service members for unusual or extended assignments, including involuntary extensions in combat zones, voluntary tour extensions for members with critical skills, and service in certain designated locations. AIP is capped at $3,000 per month and is taxable unless earned in a designated combat zone; specific locations and durations are governed by a DoD Duration of Authority table that is updated periodically. Current AIP tables and duration-of-authority information are available through DFAS.

konular.baslik: VA Blind and Low Vision Rehabilitation
konular.ozet: VA provides blind and low vision rehabilitation services to all honorably discharged veterans with significant vision loss, regardless of whether the vision loss is service-connected. Services span a continuum of care from outpatient low vision clinics to inpatient Blind Rehabilitation Centers, and include vision-enhancing devices, assistive technology training, orientation and mobility instruction, and counseling. Veterans should contact the Visual Impairment Services Team (VIST) Coordinator at their nearest VA medical center to begin an assessment.

DoDEA Virtual High School (DVHS) is a tuition-free, fully accredited online high school operated by the Department of Defense Education Activity for military-connected students who are enrolled in or eligible to attend a DoDEA brick-and-mortar school in the same geographic area. DVHS offers asynchronous online courses taught by full-time DoDEA-certified teachers, helping students maintain academic continuity during PCS moves by allowing course credits to transfer when relocating to another DoDEA school zone. Students must first enroll at their local DoDEA school and then register for DVHS courses at dvhs.dodea.edu.

Purple Heart recipients are automatically assigned to VA Priority Group 3, entitling them to VA inpatient and outpatient healthcare without copayments (a $2 copayment per 30-day supply applies only to non-service-connected prescriptions). Recipients are also eligible for Post-9/11 GI Bill benefits, the Yellow Ribbon Program, commissary and exchange shopping privileges, and potential VA home loan funding fee exemptions if receiving disability compensation. VA disability claims filed by Purple Heart recipients receive priority processing through the Veterans Benefits Administration.

Active-duty birth parents receive 6 weeks of non-chargeable maternity leave, plus up to 6 additional weeks if designated as the primary caregiver; non-primary caregivers (the other new parent) receive 21 days of non-chargeable parental leave for birth, adoption, or other qualifying events — none of which count against the service member's annual leave balance. This expanded parental leave policy applies across all branches of service for active-duty members and is separate from any convalescent leave a physician may authorize for childbirth recovery. Service members should contact their installation's Military Personnel Section or Family Support Center for service-specific request forms and approval authorities.

konular.baslik: Military Protective Order (MPO) & Domestic Abuse Resources
konular.ozet: A Military Protective Order (MPO) is issued by a military commander and can immediately restrict an active-duty service member from contacting a victim, entering a shared residence, or approaching specific locations; victims must file an unrestricted report of domestic abuse for the commander to issue one. Victims may also seek a Civilian Protective Order (CPO) from a civilian court, which applies to both active-duty and civilian abusers and is enforceable on and off base. To locate a domestic abuse victim advocate on or near your installation, use the Domestic Abuse Victim Advocate Locator at militaryonesource.mil, or call the National Domestic Violence Hotline at 1-800-799-7233.

konular.baslik: Citizenship for Children Abroad: Form N-600K (Military Parent)
konular.ozet: A U.S. citizen parent on active military duty can apply for their child's citizenship using Form N-600K even while the entire family is stationed outside the United States, as long as the child resides abroad with the service member under official military orders and has not yet turned 18. The military parent's time served abroad on official orders counts toward the physical presence requirement, waiving the usual need for the child to demonstrate time inside the United States. Applications must be filed at least 90 days before the requested interview date; USCIS will schedule the naturalization interview and oath ceremony at the overseas location.

konular.baslik: Reentry Permit (Form I-131): Protect Your Green Card During Long Deployments
konular.ozet: A lawful permanent resident (LPR) service member who will be outside the United States for more than one year should file Form I-131 before departing to obtain a Reentry Permit, which protects their green card status from being considered abandoned during the extended absence. LPRs traveling on official U.S. Government orders qualify for a 2-year permit and will not have their LPR status deemed abandoned solely due to the duration of their absence while the permit remains valid. USCIS requires the applicant to be physically present in the United States at the time Form I-131 is filed, so this should be arranged before the deployment begins.

konular.baslik: Form N-470: Preserving Naturalization Residence During Overseas Military Service
konular.ozet: Lawful permanent residents serving in the U.S. Armed Forces abroad can file Form N-470 (Application to Preserve Residence for Naturalization Purposes) to ensure that extended overseas duty does not break the continuous residence requirement needed to qualify for naturalization. For military LPRs, any time spent abroad on official U.S. military orders counts as physical presence in the United States, and the application may be filed before or after overseas service begins — but before a full year abroad has elapsed. The applicant must have lived in the United States as an LPR for at least one continuous year before applying.

konular.baslik: ImmVets: DHS Program for Noncitizen Veterans Who Were Deported
konular.ozet: The Department of Homeland Security's ImmVets initiative allows noncitizen military veterans who were removed (deported) from the United States to request parole consideration on a case-by-case basis to return and access legal counsel and the veterans' benefits they earned through service. DHS, ICE, USCIS, and CBP conduct case-by-case reviews of removal orders involving noncitizen veterans and their immediate family members; VA healthcare and other VA benefits remain accessible to eligible veterans regardless of current immigration or removal status. Veterans or family members seeking assistance can submit a request through the ImmVets portal at dhs.gov/immvets.

A VA-backed cash-out refinance loan lets eligible veterans and service members replace their current mortgage — including a non-VA loan — with a new VA-backed loan and withdraw up to 100 percent of the home's appraised value as cash for purposes such as debt repayment, home improvements, or education. To qualify, you must have a VA Certificate of Eligibility, meet the private lender's credit and income standards, and intend to occupy the home being refinanced; the loan is obtained through a private bank or mortgage company, not directly through VA. Closing costs, including the VA funding fee, apply and can typically be rolled into the loan amount.

konular.baslik: VA Construction Loan: Build a New Home
konular.ozet: The VA guaranty benefit can be used for construction loans that enable eligible veterans to build a custom home from the ground up with no down payment required; veterans with a service-connected disability may qualify for a VA funding fee exemption. Once approved, loan proceeds are placed in an escrow draw account and disbursed to the builder at specific construction milestones, with the veteran written approval required before each disbursement — projects typically take one year or more to complete. Veterans must find a VA-approved lender that specifically offers construction loan products (not all do) and a licensed local builder who can obtain required permits.
konular.kaynak_adi: VA News (U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs)

konular.ozet: When closing a VA purchase loan or IRRRL, veterans can add up to $6,000 to the loan amount to finance energy-efficient home improvements such as solar heating systems, insulation, storm windows, heat pumps, and water heater upgrades — the EEM is not a separate loan product but is added to and closed simultaneously with the primary VA loan. Improvements up to $3,000 require documentation of bid or contract costs; for amounts between $3,000 and $6,000, projected monthly utility savings must equal or exceed the increase in the monthly mortgage payment. Veterans should confirm their VA-approved lender offers EEM products, as not all do; lenders can reference Chapter 7 of the VA Lenders Handbook for the full program guidelines.

konular.baslik: TDY Per Diem: Lodging and Meal Allowances During Temporary Duty
konular.ozet: Service members on Temporary Duty (TDY) orders away from their permanent duty station are entitled to a daily per diem allowance covering lodging, meals, and incidental expenses (M&IE) based on the specific TDY location, as set by the Defense Travel Management Office (DTMO). The FY 2026 standard CONUS rate is $110 for lodging and $63 for meals and incidentals; locality-specific higher rates apply in expensive cities, and TDY assignments lasting 31 to 180 consecutive days at the same location are reimbursed at 75 percent of the applicable rate. Current rates for specific cities can be looked up at travel.dod.mil using the Per Diem Rate Lookup tool; all TDY entitlements are governed by the Joint Travel Regulations (JTR).

A pay allotment is a recurring automatic deduction from a service member pay directed to a designated account or payee — such as a personal savings account, a family member bank account, TSP contributions, or insurance premiums — processed before the member receives their net pay each month. Allotments are set up, changed, or stopped through myPay at mypay.dfas.mil, DFAS secure online pay management portal, which is accessible 24 hours a day. Because allotments are deducted before net pay is distributed, they are a reliable way to ensure recurring financial obligations are met automatically during deployment or other periods when personal access to finances may be limited.

When executing a Permanent Change of Station (PCS) move, the military provides a household goods (HHG) weight allowance — the maximum poundage the government will ship at no cost — that increases with rank and depends on dependency status. Service members are also authorized to ship up to 2,000 pounds of professional gear at no cost, with 500 pounds available for an accompanying spouse professional gear; any weight exceeding the authorized limit is billed to the member after delivery. All shipments are arranged through move.mil, the official DoD personal property site; the Joint Travel Regulations (JTR) governs all HHG entitlements, and members should contact their local transportation office to confirm rank-specific weight allowances and OCONUS or retirement move variations.

konular.baslik: VA Loan Entitlement and Loan Limits
konular.ozet: VA loan entitlement is the dollar amount the VA guarantees to repay lenders if a borrower defaults. Basic entitlement covers loans up to $144,000; bonus (second-tier) entitlement covers 25% of the lender's approved amount for larger loans. Veterans with full entitlement—no active VA loans and no unresolved prior default—face no VA-imposed loan cap and can borrow up to conforming loan limits, or higher in high-cost counties, with no down payment required.

A VA-backed purchase loan can be used to buy a manufactured (mobile) home, a lot for a manufactured home, or both, with no down payment required when the purchase price does not exceed the appraised value. Manufactured homes must meet VA minimum property requirements and be installed by a licensed installer; special underwriting guidelines apply compared to site-built homes. Eligible veterans and service members pay no private mortgage insurance and can access competitive rates through VA-approved private lenders.

An eligible buyer—veteran or non-veteran—can assume an existing VA-backed home loan by taking over the seller's remaining balance and payments, subject to lender credit and income approval and a VA funding fee. If the assuming buyer is a qualifying veteran with sufficient entitlement, they can substitute their entitlement for the seller's, fully restoring the original veteran's VA loan benefit. Without entitlement substitution, the seller's entitlement stays tied to the assumed loan until it is paid off.

Under INA 319(b), the lawful permanent resident spouse of a U.S. citizen service member on overseas orders for at least one year may apply for naturalization without meeting the standard three-year continuous residence or physical presence requirements. The applicant must be authorized to accompany their spouse abroad per official orders, must appear in the U.S. for the interview and oath, and must declare intent to reside with their service member spouse. This path is separate from INA 319(a), which applies to spouses residing continuously in the U.S. for three years.

konular.baslik: Form N-426: Certifying Military Service for Naturalization
konular.ozet: Service members currently serving in the U.S. armed forces who apply for naturalization under INA 328 or 329 must file Form N-426 (Request for Certification of Military or Naval Service) alongside their Form N-400, with the certification signed by authorized military personnel. Separated veterans are no longer required to submit N-426 and instead provide a copy of their DD-214, NGB Form 22, or other official discharge document. As of June 1, 2026, USCIS accepts only the 03/13/26 edition of Form N-426.

Under INA 329A, a noncitizen who served honorably on active duty during a designated period of military hostilities and died from an injury or disease incurred or aggravated in service may receive posthumous U.S. citizenship. A qualifying next of kin—spouse, parent, child, or sibling—must file Form N-644 (Application for Posthumous Citizenship) within two years of the service member's death; upon approval, USCIS issues Form N-645, a Certificate of Citizenship effective as of the date of death. The certificate can enable surviving family members to apply for immigration benefits as immediate relatives or under INA 319(d).

konular.baslik: VAWA Self-Petition for Immigrant Military Spouses Experiencing Abuse
konular.ozet: Under the Violence Against Women Act (VAWA), the immigrant spouse, child, or parent of a U.S. citizen or lawful permanent resident who has experienced battery or extreme cruelty may file Form I-360 as a VAWA self-petitioner to seek lawful permanent residence without the abuser's knowledge or participation. There is no filing fee for Form I-360 as a VAWA self-petitioner, and USCIS handles all cases confidentially to protect the petitioner's safety. This protection is particularly relevant to immigrant military family members who may be stationed abroad or isolated from support networks.

konular.baslik: Personally Procured Move (PPM/DITY): Keep the Savings on Your PCS
konular.ozet: A Personally Procured Move (PPM, formerly DITY) allows a service member on PCS orders to arrange their own household goods transportation and receive reimbursement at 100% of the Government Constructed Cost—what the government would have paid a contracted mover—keeping any unspent funds as an incentive. Accurate empty and loaded weight tickets are required to establish the reimbursable weight, and service members have 45 days from the planned move start date to submit all documentation through the Defense Personal Property System (DPS). Reimbursement includes a fuel surcharge and applicable accessorial fees.

konular.baslik: VA Minimum Property Requirements (MPR) for Home Loans
konular.ozet: Before a VA-backed loan closes, a VA-approved appraiser must evaluate the property to confirm it meets Minimum Property Requirements (MPRs)—standards ensuring the home is safe, sound, and sanitary—and provide an independent opinion of value. If the property does not appraise at the purchase price, the borrower may request a reconsideration of value, renegotiate the price with the seller, or pay the difference out of pocket. The VA appraisal is not a home inspection; buyers are strongly encouraged to hire a separate independent inspector to assess the property's condition.

konular.baslik: I-751 Conditional Green Card: Military Waivers and Deployment Accommodations
konular.ozet: Conditional permanent residents who qualify for naturalization under INA 329 (military service during hostilities) are not required to file Form I-751 (Petition to Remove Conditions on Residence) before their naturalization can be approved—they may naturalize while their conditional status remains in place. Service members deployed abroad may file Form I-751 without their own signature by including a copy of official orders or a commanding officer letter; the form must be marked "ACTIVE MILITARY" or "GOVERNMENT ORDERS" at the top. Conditional residents who cannot file jointly due to an abusive spouse may also apply for a waiver using Form I-751.

konular.baslik: VA Loan Closing Costs and Seller Concessions
konular.ozet: On a VA-backed home loan, most closing costs—such as appraisal fees and title insurance—are negotiated between the buyer, seller, and lender; seller concessions are capped at no more than 4% of the home's reasonable value. VA loans do not require monthly private mortgage insurance (PMI), which can substantially reduce ongoing housing costs compared to conventional loans. Borrowers should request a Loan Estimate from their lender to review all applicable fees before committing.

konular.baslik: Buying a Condo with a VA Loan: Project Approval Requirements
konular.ozet: Veterans using a VA-backed purchase loan can buy a condominium unit, but only in a project that has been independently reviewed and approved by the VA—the VA no longer accepts HUD/FHA approval as a substitute for its own review. Buyers and lenders can check whether a specific condo project is VA-approved using the VA's Loan Guaranty (LGY) condo report tool before making an offer on a unit. Approved condo purchases qualify for the full VA loan benefit package: no down payment, no monthly mortgage insurance, and competitive interest rates.

konular.baslik: VA Loan Occupancy Requirements and Military Exceptions
konular.ozet: VA-backed home loans require borrowers to certify a good-faith intent to personally occupy the property as their primary residence, with a standard move-in timeframe of within 60 days of closing. Active-duty service members who are deployed or assigned elsewhere may qualify for an extended occupancy timeline, and a spouse may satisfy the occupancy requirement on the veteran's behalf when the service member cannot be present. The VA applies a reasonable-basis test: if clear intent to occupy exists, the certification is valid even when immediate move-in is not possible.

konular.baslik: BAH for National Guard and Reserve Members on Active-Duty Orders
konular.ozet: National Guard and Reserve members are not entitled to Basic Allowance for Housing (BAH) during Inactive Duty Training (IDT) such as weekend drill, but become entitled to BAH when called to active duty on orders. Once activated, BAH is calculated at the same rates as regular active-duty members—based on pay grade, duty station location, and dependent status—and payments are processed automatically by DFAS. Service members should confirm their family members are enrolled in DEERS and verify their BAH entitlement with their unit's finance office at the start of activation.

konular.baslik: Home Sale Tax Exclusion: Military PCS Suspension Period
konular.ozet: Under IRS Section 121, homeowners can exclude up to $250,000 ($500,000 married filing jointly) in capital gains when selling a primary residence they have lived in for at least two of the previous five years. Qualifying military members may elect to suspend this five-year test period for up to 10 additional years while on qualified official extended duty—stationed at least 50 miles from the home or residing in government housing under orders for more than 90 days or indefinitely. Military members who PCS frequently may still qualify for the exclusion by meeting the two-year use requirement within the extended 15-year window.

konular.baslik: SCRA 6% Interest Rate Cap on Pre-Service Debts
konular.ozet: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (SCRA) caps interest rates at 6% on debts—including credit cards, car loans, some student loans, and business obligations—incurred before entering active duty; creditors must forgive the interest above 6% and may not charge additional fees. For mortgages, the 6% cap continues for one full year after the service member leaves active duty, providing protection during the transition to civilian life. The reduction is not automatic: service members must notify each lender in writing and include a copy of their active-duty orders to invoke the cap.

konular.baslik: USCIS Filing Fee Exemptions for Active-Duty Service Members
konular.ozet: Current and former U.S. armed forces members who served honorably on active duty are exempt from USCIS filing fees for Form I-485 (Adjustment of Status), Form I-765 (Employment Authorization), Form I-131 (Travel Documents), and Form I-360 (special immigrant/VAWA petitions)—no separate fee waiver form is required. Service members and veterans applying for naturalization under INA 328 or 329 pay no fee for Form N-400. USCIS may require documentation of military service to verify the exemption at the time of filing.

konular.baslik: FMLA Military Caregiver Leave: 26 Weeks to Care for an Injured Service Member
konular.ozet: Under the Family and Medical Leave Act (FMLA), an eligible employee who is the spouse, child, parent, or next of kin of a covered service member with a serious injury or illness may take up to 26 workweeks of unpaid leave within a single 12-month period to provide care. A covered service member includes current members of the Armed Forces (including National Guard and Reserves) who are undergoing medical treatment, recuperation, or therapy, or are on the temporary disability retired list. Military caregiver leave may be repeated if the same service member sustains a different qualifying serious injury or illness in a later period.

Any active-duty service member may request an interest-free advance of pay equal to one month of basic pay (less deductions) to help cover upfront costs of a Permanent Change of Station move; up to three months of basic pay may be authorized when additional financial need is documented and justified. The advance is processed through the service member's installation finance office and must be repaid through future paycheck deductions. Advance pay helps bridge the gap between moving costs incurred at departure and PCS reimbursements and allowances received later.

konular.baslik: VA Loan for Multi-Family Properties (Up to 4 Units)
konular.ozet: A VA-backed purchase loan can be used to buy a residential property with up to four units—including duplexes, triplexes, and four-plexes—as long as the veteran intends to personally occupy the property. Living in one unit while renting out the remaining units allows eligible veterans and service members to offset housing costs with rental income. The full VA loan benefits apply: no down payment when the purchase price does not exceed the appraised value, no monthly mortgage insurance, and competitive interest rates.

konular.baslik: Overseas Military Naturalization: USCIS Services at Military Installations Abroad
konular.ozet: USCIS provides overseas naturalization services at four hub locations for eligible service members stationed abroad: Camp Humphreys (South Korea), Commander Fleet Activities Yokosuka (Japan), U.S. Army Garrison Stuttgart (Germany), and Naval Support Activity Naples (Italy). Eligible service members applying under INA 328 or 329 may complete the entire naturalization process—interviews, filings, and oath ceremonies—without returning to the United States; USCIS also conducts ceremonies at U.S. embassies, consulates, and many other military installations. Since September 2002, more than 92,000 military members have been naturalized, including nearly 11,000 who received citizenship overseas.

konular.baslik: USCIS Expedite Requests for Pending Deployments
konular.ozet: Service members with a pending USCIS application who receive deployment orders may request expedited processing by writing "I have an upcoming deployment" in bold on the first page of the application or a cover sheet at the time of filing. For applications already submitted, service members can contact the USCIS Military Help Line at 1-877-CIS-4MIL (1-877-247-4645) to flag the pending deployment. USCIS evaluates expedite requests case by case using criteria including pending deployment, urgent humanitarian situations, and national interest; approval is not automatic and military spouses are not automatically granted expedited processing.

konular.baslik: VA Coverage for Emergency Care at Non-VA Facilities
konular.ozet: VA-enrolled veterans who need emergency care may receive coverage or reimbursement at non-VA emergency facilities if they meet the prudent layperson standard; for non-service-connected conditions, the veteran must have received VA or in-network community care within the previous 24 months. The treating provider must notify the VA within 72 hours of when emergency care begins, though late notification does not automatically disqualify reimbursement. VA coverage extends only until the veteran can be safely transferred to a VA or in-network facility, and the VA will not cover costs that another insurer denied due to the veteran's failure to follow the insurer's procedures.

konular.baslik: Military Spouse Federal Hiring: Noncompetitive Appointment Authority
konular.ozet: Under Executive Order 13473 and FY 2023 NDAA amendments, spouses of active-duty service members are eligible for noncompetitive appointment to federal civilian positions through a special hiring authority—allowing agencies to hire them outside the competitive examining process. The 2023 NDAA expanded eligibility to all spouses of active-duty members regardless of whether they are relocating on PCS orders; the authority is in effect through December 31, 2028. This authority does not guarantee an appointment over other applicants, but provides a significant pathway into federal employment for military families.

The Exceptional Family Member Program (EFMP) is a mandatory enrollment program for active-duty service members who have a dependent with special medical, mental health, or educational needs; enrollment ensures that assignment locations can support those needs before permanent change of station orders are issued. The program provides installation-based and community-based support services including special needs consultants, respite care, early intervention, and caregiver support. Families can access EFMP support by contacting their installation Family Support Center or calling Military OneSource at 800-342-9647.

konular.baslik: Military Child Care Fee Assistance Programs
konular.ozet: When on-installation Child Development Centers (CDCs) are full or unavailable, eligible military families can receive fee assistance to offset the cost of civilian community child care through the Military Child Care in Your Neighborhood (MCCYN) program. MCCYN-PLUS is available in more than 20 states through Child Care Aware of America; a Child Care in Your Home pilot assists eligible families in 12 high-demand locations. On-installation CDCs serve children ages 6 weeks to 5 years and operate year-round; all DoD-approved programs must make reasonable accommodations for children with disabilities.

Service members who reside in single-type government quarters and have a court order to pay child support are entitled to Basic Allowance for Housing Differential (BAH-DIFF), which equals the difference between the BAH with-dependents rate and the BAH without-dependents rate for their grade and duty station. Members not residing in government quarters who pay court-ordered child support are entitled to the full BAH with-dependents rate rather than BAH-DIFF. The BAH-DIFF entitlement was restructured in 2006 and applies only to those actually living in government-provided single-type quarters.

konular.baslik: VA Home Loan with Non-Veteran Co-Borrower
konular.ozet: VA home loans may be made jointly with a non-veteran co-borrower under 38 C.F.R. 36.4307, but the VA guaranty applies only to the veteran's proportional share of the total loan amount; the non-veteran's portion carries no VA guarantee and lenders may require a down payment or additional security for that share. For funding fee purposes, the loan amount is divided equally among all borrowers and the fee is calculated only on the veteran's portion. This arrangement is separate from the veteran-plus-veteran joint loan option, where each veteran's entitlement covers their respective share.

Unlike most conventional loans, VA underwriting requires borrowers to meet a minimum residual income standard: gross monthly income minus estimated taxes, ongoing debts, and the proposed housing payment must equal or exceed a threshold that varies by geographic region (Northeast, Midwest, South, or West) and household size. This residual income check ensures the family has enough cash flow for daily living expenses after meeting all obligations. A borrower whose debt-to-income ratio exceeds 41% can still qualify if residual income surpasses the regional table minimum by at least 20%.

Family Separation Allowance (FSA) is additional monthly pay for service members whose family members cannot live with or near them at their permanent duty station, available at both domestic and overseas locations. Eligibility applies to active-duty members, National Guard, and Reserve personnel when government housing or transportation for dependents is unavailable due to military orders. Service members must submit DD Form 1561 to their installation's Personnel Support Office; current monthly rates are published by the Defense Finance and Accounting Service (DFAS).

TRICARE Prime requires a referral from your Primary Care Manager (PCM) and prior authorization from your regional contractor before receiving specialty care; receiving specialty care without a referral means paying out of pocket. Other TRICARE plans — including TRICARE Select and Reserve Select — generally do not require referrals except for applied behavioral analysis, though pre-authorization is still required for transplants, home health, hospice, and certain other services. Referral and authorization status can be checked through the Humana Military portal (East Region) or TriWest portal (West Region).

VA Home Based Primary Care (HBPC) delivers medical services in veterans' homes for those with serious ongoing conditions who have difficulty attending clinic appointments due to illness severity or homebound status. The interdisciplinary team provides in-home visits by physicians or nurse practitioners, along with nursing, social work, rehabilitation, psychological, nutritional, and pharmacy services. All enrolled veterans who meet clinical need may qualify; contact a VA social worker at your nearest VA medical center to determine local availability and enrollment.

Veterans and eligible next of kin (spouse, parent, child, or sibling) can obtain free copies of military service records — including the DD Form 214, Official Military Personnel File (OMPF), medical records, and replacement medals — through the National Personnel Records Center (NPRC). The fastest method is online via the eVetRecs system at vetrecs.archives.gov using ID.me identity verification; requests can also be submitted by mailing or faxing a signed Standard Form 180 to NPRC, 1 Archives Drive, St. Louis, MO 63138. Processing typically takes 90 or more days; records become publicly available 62 years after a veteran's separation.

Surviving spouses, children, and parents of service members who died in the line of duty or from a service-connected cause may qualify for VA benefits including Dependency and Indemnity Compensation (DIC), Survivors Pension, education assistance through Chapter 35 or the Fry Scholarship, VA-backed home loans, burial benefits, and family life insurance options. The VA Benefits Hotline (800-827-1000) and the Caregiver Support Line (855-260-3274) provide personalized guidance; installation Casualty Assistance Officers and Military OneSource also offer support during the transition period. Survivors should inquire about Gold Star lapel pins, base access privileges, and commissary and exchange eligibility through their branch casualty office.

The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (SCRA) caps the interest rate on mortgages taken out before entering active duty at 6 percent for the duration of service, with the reduced rate continuing for one year after separation. No foreclosure or seizure of property for nonpayment of a pre-service mortgage is valid if made during active duty or within nine months after active duty ends, unless carried out under a valid court order. Service members who miss a mortgage payment due to military service should contact their installation's legal assistance office immediately to invoke these protections.

State Veterans Homes are long-term care facilities owned and operated by state governments, offering nursing home care, domiciliary (residential) services, or adult day health care to eligible veterans; eligibility requirements and costs vary by state, and some homes also admit non-veteran surviving spouses and Gold Star parents. All 50 states and Puerto Rico have facilities; VA certifies and annually inspects them to ensure quality standards, and provides financial support to help defray veteran care costs. A searchable directory of facilities is maintained by the National Association of State Veterans Homes at nasvh.org/directory.

TRICARE covers a full range of substance use disorder treatment including inpatient hospitalization, intensive outpatient programs, medically supervised detoxification, medication-assisted treatment for opioid addiction (such as methadone and buprenorphine), partial hospitalization programs, and office-based opioid treatment; coverage excludes aversion therapy and unproven treatments, and all covered services must be medically necessary. Active-duty service members seeking substance use treatment should be aware that some services — including specialized substance abuse therapy — require a referral even under plans that otherwise do not mandate them. Contact your regional TRICARE contractor (Humana Military for the East Region, TriWest for the West Region) for specific prior authorization requirements and coverage limits.

The VA provides comprehensive substance use disorder treatment for veterans struggling with alcohol, opioids, tobacco, or other drugs, including medically supervised detoxification, medication-based therapies (methadone and buprenorphine for opioids, nicotine replacement), outpatient and residential counseling, intensive treatment programs, family counseling, and specialized programs for women veterans and homeless veterans. Co-occurring mental health conditions such as PTSD and depression are treated alongside substance use disorders. Veterans should apply for VA health care to access these services; those not yet enrolled can reach an SUD program at 800-827-1000 or visit a local Vet Center (available to combat zone veterans without VA enrollment).

The VA Fully Developed Claims (FDC) program allows veterans to potentially receive faster disability benefit decisions by submitting all supporting evidence — private medical records, military treatment records, and relevant personnel documents — simultaneously with VA Form 21-526EZ, along with a certification that no additional evidence is needed. Veterans must attend any scheduled C&P exams; submitting additional materials after filing removes the claim from FDC and converts it to standard processing. Filing through FDC carries no risk: if the VA determines it needs additional federal records, the claim converts to standard processing without penalty and with no effect on the claim's attention or potential benefits.

The TRICARE Chiropractic Health Care Program covers chiropractic services only for active duty service members and activated National Guard and Reserve members at designated military hospitals and clinics. Family members, retirees, their family members, unremarried former spouses, and survivors are not eligible for this program. Those who do not qualify may be referred to alternative Military Health System services such as physical therapy or orthopedics.

TRICARE covers physical therapy when professionally administered to aid recovery from disease or injury, including services from licensed physical therapists, physical therapist assistants, occupational therapists, and certain nurse practitioners or podiatrists. Covered therapy must be medically necessary and designed to improve mobility, strength, coordination, or endurance. TRICARE does not cover maintenance therapy, general exercise programs, chiropractic services, acupuncture, or passive range-of-motion exercises unrelated to restoring a specific loss of function.

TRICARE covers occupational therapy (OT) when prescribed and supervised by a physician, certified physician assistant, nurse practitioner, or podiatrist and professionally administered to improve, restore, or maintain function. Services must be medically necessary and evidence-based. TRICARE does not cover vocational assessment and training, general exercise programs, maintenance therapy that no longer requires skilled intervention, or repetitive exercise for gait and endurance.

TRICARE covers a wide range of clinical preventive services including blood pressure and cholesterol screenings, cancer screenings, immunizations for all age groups, well-child and well-woman exams, tobacco cessation services, hepatitis B and C and HPV testing, and parent and patient health education. Pediatric lead level screening and newborn care are also included. Contact TRICARE or visit tricare.mil for the complete list of covered preventive services.

TRICARE covers hearing aids for active duty service members and eligible dependents who meet audiological criteria — adults must have a hearing threshold of at least 40 dB HL in one or both ears or a speech recognition score below 94%; children must have a threshold of at least 26 dB HL. Retired service members are not covered for hearing aids under TRICARE but may access VA hearing programs or the Retiree-At-Cost Hearing Aid Program (RACHAP) at military hospitals. Children of retired sponsors may qualify if enrolled in TRICARE Prime or US Family Health Plan and the child meets hearing loss criteria.

konular.baslik: VA Prosthetic and Sensory Aids Service
konular.ozet: The VA Prosthetic and Sensory Aids Service (PSAS) provides eligible veterans with prosthetic and orthotic devices, hearing aids, automobile adaptive equipment (AAE) for vehicle modifications, home improvements and structural alterations (HISA) for accessibility, service dog veterinary health benefits, clothing allowances, and assistive technology solutions. Veterans enrolled in VA health care can access these services by contacting their local VA medical center or using the VA facility locator tool. The program provides commercially available advanced medical devices and products to maximize veteran independence.

konular.baslik: Consular Report of Birth Abroad for Military Families
konular.ozet: A Consular Report of Birth Abroad (CRBA) is an official U.S. State Department document that records U.S. citizenship at birth for children under 18 born overseas to at least one U.S. citizen parent, including military families stationed abroad. Parents apply through their nearest U.S. embassy or consulate using MyTravelGov; Form DS-5507 may be required when one parent is not a U.S. citizen, is absent during application, or the child was born out of wedlock to a U.S. citizen father. A CRBA is not a birth certificate, but it serves as proof of U.S. citizenship for the child.

Military OneSource provides free, short-term, confidential counseling to active duty service members, National Guard and Reserve members regardless of activation status, their immediate family members, and eligible surviving spouses and children for up to 365 days post-separation. Sessions are available in-person, by phone, secure video, or online chat and cover relationship conflicts, stress management, parenting issues, grief, and deployment-related challenges. Information is not reported to the chain of command or security clearance investigations; reach counselors 24/7 at 800-342-9647.

TRICARE covers part-time and intermittent skilled nursing care, home health aide services, physical therapy, speech therapy, occupational therapy, and medical social services when pre-authorized by your regional TRICARE contractor. Services must be delivered by a participating home health agency; separate charges may apply for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, and certain medications. Beneficiaries who need more care than standard TRICARE covers may be eligible for additional support through the Extended Care Health Option (ECHO) Home Health Care program.

TRICARE covers durable medical equipment (DME) prescribed by a physician when it serves a medical purpose, can withstand repeated use, and would not be useful without an illness or injury. Covered items include equipment that improves, restores, or maximizes patient function, along with necessary customizations, accessories, repairs, and replacements when conditions change. TRICARE does not cover equipment available from military facilities, items with luxury features, routine servicing not required by the manufacturer, expendable items such as pads or bandages, or non-medical equipment like humidifiers or exercise bikes.

MilitaryINSTALLATIONS is a free searchable directory provided by Military OneSource that helps service members learn about their duty station, covering installation overviews, check-in procedures, and local community resources. Users can search by installation name, state, specific programs, or ZIP code to find housing offices, child development centers, family advocacy services, medical facilities, schools, financial services, and recreation programs. For personalized support, reach Military OneSource at 800-342-9647 or via secure chat.

konular.baslik: VA Free Mental Health Care for Active Duty Members
konular.ozet: Active duty service members can access free mental health care at any VA medical center or Vet Center without enrolling in VA health care — no eligibility verification or enrollment is required. Walk-in access is available 24/7 at VA medical centers; Vet Centers are available during clinic hours; or call 877-222-8387 (Monday through Friday, 8 a.m. to 8 p.m. ET). Service members may also receive routine VA care with a valid TRICARE referral at facilities with VA/DoD sharing agreements, and can apply for full VA health care enrollment as soon as they receive separation or retirement orders.

konular.baslik: Brandon Act: Right to Request Mental Health Evaluation
konular.ozet: The Brandon Act (signed December 27, 2021, as part of the 2022 NDAA) gives service members the right to request a mental health evaluation at any time, for any reason, and in any environment by asking a supervisor of E-6 grade or above — no reason or basis is required. Confidentiality is protected as much as possible under federal privacy laws and Department of War regulations. The law was named after Navy Petty Officer 3rd Class Brandon Caserta, who died by suicide in 2018, and aims to reduce stigma around seeking mental health care.

konular.baslik: REACH-Spouse Mental Health Training for Military Spouses
konular.ozet: REACH-Spouse (Resources Exist, Asking Can Help-Spouse) is a free, facilitator-led two-session training program offered through Military OneSource that equips military spouses to recognize mental health challenges and connect themselves and their service members with support resources. Session 2 teaches the Question, Persuade, Refer (QPR) technique for suicide prevention, enabling spouses to respond effectively when a service member may be struggling. Field testing found that attendees leave with significantly more knowledge of available resources.

konular.baslik: Military Spouse Employment Partnership and SpouseWorks
konular.ozet: SpouseWorks (formerly the Spouse Education and Career Opportunities/SECO program) provides free career coaching, resume writing, interview preparation, and job search assistance through MySECO counselors. Additional free resources include 5,000+ Udemy courses, a one-year FlexJobs subscription for remote/flexible jobs, and a paid 12-week Career Accelerator fellowship program. The Military Spouse Employment Partnership (MSEP) connects spouses to 600+ private-sector employers committed to hiring military spouses; access all services at spouseworks.militaryonesource.mil or by calling 800-342-9647.

konular.baslik: CFPB Financial Protections for Service Members
konular.ozet: The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) enforces financial protections for service members including the Military Lending Act (MLA), which caps interest and fees on most consumer loans, and the Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (SCRA), which guards against wrongful foreclosures and repossessions. The CFPB also provides resources on scams targeting service members, monitors complaints from the military community, and takes enforcement action against violators. Service members can file complaints or get help at consumerfinance.gov or by calling 855-411-2372.

The Armed Forces Retirement Home (AFRH) is an independent federal agency operating two residential campuses for eligible enlisted veterans — one in Washington, D.C. and one in Gulfport, Mississippi. Residents receive housing, healthcare, recreation programs, 24/7 wellness center access, nutrition guidance, physical and occupational therapy, dental care, and podiatry care. For admissions information including eligibility requirements, call 800-422-9988 or visit afrh.gov.

Veterans and eligible family members can apply in advance for a VA pre-need eligibility determination to confirm eligibility for burial in a VA national cemetery, making the process easier for families during a time of loss. Applications are submitted online, by mail using VA Form 40-10007 (to the NCA Evidence Intake Center, Janesville, WI), or by fax; providing a DD214 speeds processing. The VA will attempt to locate records if separation documents are unavailable, but cannot guarantee placement in a preferred cemetery due to space availability.

Active duty members relocating under PCS orders can deduct unreimbursed moving expenses — household goods, storage, and travel/lodging (not meals) — using Form 3903 filed with Schedule 1 of Form 1040. Expenses already reimbursed or excluded from income by the government cannot be deducted.

Service members may elect to include otherwise-excluded combat pay in their earned income when calculating the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC), potentially increasing the refundable credit amount. This election is voluntary and may only be beneficial depending on individual circumstances; IRS Publication 3 explains the rules.

Under the Joint Travel Regulations, service members are reimbursed for transporting one pet during a PCS move; additional pets are transported at personal expense. Enhanced reimbursement is available for pets moving from high-rabies-risk countries, and reservations for Patriot Express flights should be made 90–120 days in advance.

Military OneSource provides free financial counseling to service members and eligible family members through phone, video, and in-person consultations, covering debt management, savings programs, retirement planning, college funding, and consumer rights education. Counselors are available 24/7 at 800-342-9647 and can connect service members with installation-based financial educators and additional resources specific to military life.

UCX is a federal program providing unemployment benefits to former military members who served on active duty and were separated under honorable conditions; no payroll deductions are required from service members, as the military branches fund the benefits. The program is administered by state workforce agencies as agents of the federal government, so benefit amounts, duration, and specific eligibility requirements are determined by each individual state. Apply at your state workforce agency as soon as possible after separation, typically presenting your DD-214.

konular.baslik: Continued Health Care Benefit Program (CHCBP) — Post-Separation Coverage
konular.ozet: CHCBP is a premium-based temporary health coverage option that bridges military and civilian benefits when TRICARE eligibility ends, available to separated active duty members, former dependents, and retirees losing coverage. Enrollment must be completed within 60 days of losing TRICARE, and coverage lasts 18 to 36 months depending on the individual's circumstances. Benefits match TRICARE Select and include prescription coverage; contact BCBS Federal Employee Program at 888-550-6788 or visit tricare.mil to enroll.

konular.baslik: Coast Guard Mutual Assistance (CGMA) — Emergency Financial Aid
konular.ozet: Coast Guard Mutual Assistance (CGMA) provides interest-free loans, grants, financial counseling, and referrals to active duty Coast Guard members, reserve personnel, retirees, civilian employees, Public Health Service officers assigned to the Coast Guard, auxiliary members, and surviving spouses facing financial hardship or emergencies. The organization delivered over $12.1 million in assistance to more than 4,000 individuals in 2025; members can reach a local CGMA representative (over 600 available nationwide) or call 800-881-2462 for help.

konular.baslik: BRS Lump Sum Option at Retirement
konular.ozet: Under the Blended Retirement System, eligible service members retiring at 20 or more years may elect to receive either 25% or 50% of the projected value of their retirement pension as a lump sum payment at the time of separation. This election reduces monthly pension annuity payments until the retiree reaches their Full Retirement Age (67); after that point, monthly payments return to the full calculated amount. This option is separate from the TSP and is irrevocable once elected at retirement.

konular.baslik: Legacy Military Retirement — Final Pay, High-36, and CSB/REDUX
konular.ozet: Service members who entered active duty before January 1, 2018 and did not elect the Blended Retirement System are covered by one of three legacy retirement plans: Final Basic Pay (entry before September 8, 1980), High-36 (entry from September 8, 1980 through December 31, 2017 without a REDUX election), or Career Status Bonus/REDUX (entry after July 31, 1986, with acceptance of a mid-career bonus in exchange for a reduced retirement formula). All three legacy plans require a minimum of 20 years of qualifying service, and retirement pay under all plans is protected by annual cost-of-living adjustments.

Naturalization applicants who have a physical or developmental disability or mental impairment that prevents them from meeting the English language or civics requirements may file Form N-648, Medical Certification for Disability Exceptions, along with their N-400. The form must be certified by a licensed physician, doctor of osteopathy, or clinical psychologist within 180 days before the N-400 is submitted to USCIS. There is no filing fee for Form N-648, though the certifying medical professional may charge for their examination.

konular.baslik: Special Immigrant Green Card for Treaty Force Members (Form I-360)
konular.ozet: Noncitizen members of the U.S. Armed Forces who enlisted outside the United States under a treaty or agreement in effect on October 1, 1991, and served honorably on active duty since October 15, 1978, may petition for lawful permanent resident status using Form I-360 after accumulating 12 years of aggregate active-duty service — or 6 years with a reenlistment commitment totaling 12 years. A recommendation for special immigrant classification from the relevant military department is also required. This pathway primarily applies to nationals of countries with longstanding military service agreements with the United States.

konular.baslik: Deferred Action for Military Family Members
konular.ozet: USCIS may grant deferred action — temporary relief from removal — to certain immediate family members (spouse, parent, son, or daughter) of active-duty service members, veterans, and individuals currently enlisted through the Delayed Entry Program. Deferred action is granted case-by-case for up to two years and treats the family member as lawfully present during that period; it is distinct from parole in place, which applies to individuals who entered the U.S. without inspection. Individuals already in formal removal proceedings are generally not eligible for USCIS-granted deferred action.

Lawful permanent residents who served honorably in the U.S. Armed Forces for at least one year — including peacetime service — may apply for naturalization under INA Section 328 using Form N-400 and a DD-214 or official discharge document; USCIS charges no filing fee. Unlike the hostilities-based path under INA §329, Section 328 requires the applicant to be a lawful permanent resident at the time of the naturalization interview and to demonstrate English language ability and knowledge of U.S. civics. Uncharacterized discharges issued on or after August 1, 2024 no longer meet the under-honorable-conditions requirement.

Veterans who have paid off a VA-guaranteed loan and sold the property can have their full VA loan entitlement restored for future use by submitting VA Form 26-1880 with proof the prior loan was paid in full. Veterans who paid off a VA loan but kept the property may restore entitlement on a one-time-only basis to purchase another primary residence. Veterans who refinanced a VA loan into a conventional loan may also restore entitlement while retaining ownership of the original home.

konular.baslik: SCRA Lease Termination for PCS and Deployment
konular.ozet: Under 50 U.S.C. § 3955, service members may terminate a residential lease early without penalty when they receive PCS orders or deployment orders lasting more than 90 days — or when they entered active duty after signing the lease and will serve at least 90 days. Written notice plus a copy of the military orders must be delivered to the landlord (by hand, private carrier, or USPS with return receipt); the lease then terminates 30 days after the first date on which the next rent payment is due following delivery of the notice. Guard and Reserve members called to active duty for 180 or more days may also terminate vehicle leases under the same statute.

Active duty service members from all branches — including Coast Guard, NOAA, and Public Health Service, plus Guard and Reserve members on continuous active duty for more than 30 days — receive dental care at no out-of-pocket cost through military dental clinics. When a military dental clinic is not available, the Active Duty Dental Program provides access to a civilian dental network through United Concordia; remote service members can self-refer for routine care after obtaining an Appointment Control Number (ACN) by calling 1-866-984-2337. Emergency dental care through ADDP does not require prior authorization.

konular.baslik: TRICARE Mental Health and Behavioral Health Coverage
konular.ozet: TRICARE covers a broad range of mental health and behavioral health services, including outpatient counseling and therapy, inpatient psychiatric care, residential treatment, and substance use disorder treatment for conditions such as depression, anxiety, PTSD, and others. No referral or prior authorization is required for outpatient mental health or substance use disorder care — beneficiaries, including active duty service members, can self-refer directly to a behavioral health provider or military clinic at any time. Active duty members can contact their installation behavioral health clinic directly without a referral from their primary care provider.

TRICARE covers all medically necessary pregnancy care, including prenatal visits, labor and delivery, and postpartum care for at least six weeks with a minimum of two follow-up visits. Hospital stays are generally 48 hours after a vaginal delivery or 96 hours after a cesarean section, with longer stays covered if medically necessary. Newborns must be registered in DEERS before TRICARE coverage can begin; enrollment should be completed as soon as possible after birth.

konular.baslik: TRICARE Coverage When Guard or Reserve Members Are Activated
konular.ozet: National Guard and Reserve members ordered to active duty for more than 30 consecutive days — and their eligible family members — receive the same TRICARE health and dental benefits as active duty service members, beginning on the first day of orders. Activated members choose from TRICARE Prime, Prime Remote, or their overseas equivalents; dental coverage automatically switches from the premium-based TRICARE Dental Program to active duty dental benefits. After deactivation, TRICARE Transitional Assistance Management Program (TAMP) provides 180 days of continued coverage.

konular.baslik: Reserve and Guard Drill Pay (Inactive Duty Training)
konular.ozet: Guard and Reserve members earn drill pay for each Inactive Duty Training (IDT) period completed, such as the weekend drills that make up most unit training schedules; a standard monthly drill weekend counts as four IDT periods. Drill pay rates follow the same pay grade and years-of-service structure as active duty basic pay and are published annually by DFAS. Most units require 48 IDT periods per year, with annual training periods compensated at the equivalent active duty daily rate.

Child Development Centers (CDCs) at military installations provide nationally accredited child care for infants through preschool-age children of service members, with fees set on a sliding scale based on total family income rather than a flat rate. CDCs offer full-day, part-day, and space-available hourly care, typically Monday through Friday; children with special needs can receive support in coordination with the Exceptional Family Member Program. Military families in any branch can search for and request spots at CDCs worldwide through MilitaryChildCare.com, the official DoD child care platform.

konular.baslik: Yellow Ribbon Reintegration Program (Guard & Reserve Families)
konular.ozet: The Department of Defense Yellow Ribbon Reintegration Program (YRRP) supports National Guard and Reserve members and their families throughout the deployment cycle by hosting pre-deployment, mid-deployment, and reintegration events at 30, 60, and 90 days after return from deployment. Events connect participants with TRICARE coordinators, Military OneSource counselors, Military Family Life Consultants, personal financial counselors, chaplains, transition advisors, and employment support through the Employer Support of the Guard and Reserve. Eligibility is for Guard and Reserve members mobilized or deployed and their families.

konular.baslik: Free Immigration Legal Services (AILA Military Assistance Program)
konular.ozet: The American Immigration Lawyers Association Military Assistance Program provides free immigration legal services — from visa petitions and naturalization to alien registration and reentry permits — to active-duty service members, reservists, veterans, and their immediate families for cases too complex for standard military legal offices. Military OneSource connects eligible individuals to AILA volunteer attorneys and can also assist with document translation and language interpretation for immigration matters. Contact Military OneSource at 800-342-9647 or use the Legal Services Locator to find the nearest installation legal assistance office.

konular.baslik: ESGR Ombudsman — Free USERRA Mediation for Guard & Reserve
konular.ozet: The Employer Support of the Guard and Reserve (ESGR) offers free, neutral mediation services to help Guard and Reserve members resolve workplace disputes with civilian employers over USERRA rights — such as reemployment, benefits continuation, or discrimination based on military service. More than 4,500 volunteer ombudsmen in all 50 states conduct informal mediation without enforcement authority; if mediation fails, cases are referred to the Department of Labor's Veterans' Employment and Training Service (VETS) for formal action. Service members can request assistance at 1-800-336-4590 (option 1) or by emailing osd.USERRA@mail.mil.

konular.baslik: Naturalization During Hostilities (INA §329): Designated Periods and Exemptions
konular.ozet: INA §329 allows service members who served honorably during a designated period of hostilities to apply for naturalization without meeting the standard continuous residence or physical presence requirements. Current designated periods include service from September 11, 2001 to the present; prior periods include the Gulf War (1990–1991), Vietnam era (1961–1978), World War II (1939–1946), and World War I (1917–1918). Applicants file Form N-400 with Form N-426 certifying service; the filing fee is waived for current members and honorably discharged veterans.

konular.baslik: Naturalization Eligibility for National Guard and Reserve Members
konular.ozet: National Guard and Selected Reserve members may qualify for naturalization under INA §328 (peacetime) or §329 (hostilities) with one year of honorable service in a federally recognized unit. Guard service must be as part of the Selected Reserve of the Ready Reserve to qualify; members on Title 10 federal activation during a designated hostilities period qualify under the more favorable §329 terms with no residence or presence requirement. Current members file Form N-426 certified by an authorized officer; former members submit DD Form 214 or NGB Form 22.

konular.baslik: Form N-600: Certificate of Citizenship for Children of Service Members
konular.ozet: Form N-600 (Application for Certificate of Citizenship) allows children who acquired or derived U.S. citizenship through a parent’s military service, naturalization, or birth abroad to obtain an official certificate proving their citizenship status. Children born abroad to a U.S. citizen military parent may have automatically acquired citizenship at birth under INA §301 or §309 and can use N-600 to document that status. The certificate is not required to exercise citizenship rights but serves as proof for passport applications, employment, and other official purposes.

konular.baslik: Expedited Naturalization Oath Ceremony Before Deployment
konular.ozet: Service members with a pending naturalization application who have an upcoming deployment may request an expedited oath ceremony by writing “I have an upcoming deployment” in bold on the cover letter or first page of their application. For applications already filed, contact the USCIS Military Help Line at 1-877-CIS-4MIL (1-877-247-4645) to request expedited scheduling. USCIS reviews each request individually; approval is not automatic, but pending deployment is a recognized basis for expedited processing.

konular.baslik: Advance Parole (Form I-131) for Service Members with Pending Green Card Applications
konular.ozet: Green card applicants with a pending Form I-485 (adjustment of status) who need to travel abroad—including for military deployment orders—must file Form I-131 to obtain advance parole before departing; traveling without it typically abandons the pending application. Form I-131 can be filed concurrently with I-485 or after; applicants should include military orders as supporting documentation when deployment is the reason for travel. For military members already abroad requesting immigration parole consideration, USCIS processes those requests through its discretionary parole program.

konular.baslik: Enlistment Authority for Lawful Permanent Residents (Green Card Holders)
konular.ozet: Lawful permanent residents (green card holders) are authorized to enlist in the U.S. Armed Forces; at Military Entrance Processing Stations (MEPS) they must present a valid Form I-551 (Permanent Resident Card) along with a valid passport or government-issued ID. Honorable service as an LPR makes a member eligible for expedited naturalization under INA §328 or §329 without the standard five-year residency requirement. Maintaining LPR status in good standing throughout service is required to preserve naturalization eligibility.

konular.baslik: Green Card Holder Returning to the U.S. After Long Overseas Deployment
konular.ozet: Lawful permanent residents returning from deployment must present a valid, unexpired Form I-551 (green card) at the port of entry; an expired card, ADIT stamp in a passport, or Form I-94 with an ADIT stamp is also accepted pending replacement. If a deployment causes absence longer than the green card’s one-year travel validity, a two-year Reentry Permit (Form I-131) obtained before departure extends travel protection; members who were unable to obtain one may apply for a Returning Resident (SB-1) immigrant visa at the nearest U.S. Embassy or Consulate. CBP officers are familiar with military deployment circumstances and long absences should be documented with official military orders.

konular.baslik: Afghan Special Immigrant Visa: Chief of Mission Approval Process
konular.ozet: Afghan nationals who served the U.S. government or ISAF mission in Afghanistan for at least one year between October 7, 2001 and December 31, 2023 may apply for an SIV through the Chief of Mission (COM) approval process using Form DS-157, submitted to the U.S. Department of State. Applicants must demonstrate faithful and valuable service and that they face or have faced a serious ongoing threat as a result of that employment; a positive recommendation letter is required. Once COM approval is granted, USCIS processes the immigrant visa petition and the applicant proceeds through consular processing or, if already in the U.S., adjustment of status.

konular.baslik: BAH Dependency Status: Who Qualifies as a Dependent and How to Update
konular.ozet: Basic Allowance for Housing rates are higher for members with dependents; qualifying dependents include a lawful spouse, an unmarried child under age 21 (or up to 23 if a full-time student), or any person for whom the member has a court-ordered child support obligation. Members who live in government quarters but pay court-ordered child support receive BAH-DIFF — the difference between the with-dependent and without-dependent rate — rather than full BAH. To add or update a dependent, report to your Installation Finance Office with DD Form 137 (series) and supporting documents such as a marriage certificate, birth certificate, or court order.

konular.baslik: Military Relocation Assistance Program (RAP): Free PCS Support Services
konular.ozet: The congressionally mandated Relocation Assistance Program (RAP) provides free services to service members and families undergoing a PCS move, delivered through each installation's Military and Family Support Center. Services include pre-departure briefings, newcomer orientations, housing referrals, household goods scheduling assistance, school liaison support, spouse employment resources, and child care referrals. Contact your installation's support center via MilitaryINSTALLATIONS at installations.militaryonesource.mil, or call Military OneSource at 800-342-9647.

konular.baslik: SCRA Vehicle Storage Protection: Lien Enforcement Requires a Court Order
konular.ozet: Under 50 U.S.C. § 3958 (SCRA), no storage facility, repair shop, or lienholder may foreclose on or sell a service member's vehicle or property to satisfy a storage, repair, or cleaning lien during active duty or within 90 days after, without first obtaining a valid court order. Courts reviewing enforcement requests may stay proceedings and adjust the service member's financial obligation as equity requires; enforcement without a court order is a federal SCRA violation that the Department of Justice has actively prosecuted. Members whose vehicle or property is threatened by a storage lien during deployment should contact their nearest military legal assistance (JAG) office.

konular.baslik: On-Base Housing Waitlist: How to Apply and What to Expect
konular.ozet: Most military installations operate waitlists for on-base (government quarters) housing, managed either by the Installation Housing Office or by a privatized housing company under the Military Housing Privatization Initiative. Priority is based on rank, number of dependents, and eligibility date — typically the day you signed out from your previous duty station. Apply as early as possible, even before PCS orders are finalized; use MilitaryINSTALLATIONS at installations.militaryonesource.mil to find your gaining installation's housing office, required forms, and current wait times.

konular.baslik: Involuntary Separation Pay (ISP): Entitlement and VA Recoupment Rules
