konular.ozet: Service members involuntarily discharged under honorable conditions after completing 6 or more but fewer than 20 years of active service may receive Involuntary Separation Pay; full ISP equals 10% of monthly basic pay multiplied by 12 months and years of service, while half ISP is 50% of that amount. Members with fewer than 6 years are generally ineligible, with the sole exception of a sole survivorship discharge. If VA disability compensation is later awarded, the VA withholds monthly payments until the after-tax ISP amount is fully recouped — a recovery that does not apply to separations resulting from combat-zone disabilities.

konular.baslik: Disability Severance Pay: Calculation, Taxes, and VA Offset Rules
konular.ozet: Service members separated with a combined disability rating below 30% receive disability severance pay equal to twice the monthly basic pay multiplied by years of creditable service (capped at 19 years), with a minimum credit of 3 years for most separations or 6 years for combat-zone disabilities. The payment is normally taxable but becomes tax-exempt if the disability resulted from armed conflict, extra-hazardous service, or if the member is entitled to VA disability compensation. VA disability payments are withheld monthly until the full severance amount is recouped — but no recoupment applies to disabilities incurred in a combat zone or combat-related operations after January 28, 2008.

konular.baslik: DFAS myPay: Manage Your Military Pay Online
konular.ozet: The DFAS myPay portal at mypay.dfas.mil allows active-duty members, reservists, and retirees to view and print Leave and Earnings Statements (LES), access W-2 and other tax documents, update direct deposit banking information, and change federal and state tax withholding elections. Members can also manage up to 15 discretionary allotments (savings, insurance, bond purchases), enroll in or adjust TSP contributions, and update their mailing address. Account access requires a Common Access Card (CAC) or a DS Logon credential.

konular.baslik: Special and Incentive Pays for Military Healthcare Professionals
konular.ozet: Military physicians, dentists, nurses, and other healthcare officers receive special pays in addition to basic pay, including Variable Special Pay (VSP), Board-Certified Pay, Additional Special Pay, and Multiyear Retention Bonuses — Medical Corps accession bonuses can reach up to $400,000 and retention bonuses up to $75,000 per year depending on specialty and service obligation length. Dental officers have analogous programs with retention bonuses up to $50,000 per year; nurse and non-physician provider officers receive incentive special pay of $2,000–$5,000 annually for board-certified specialties. Current rate tables are published annually by DFAS at dfas.mil under the Health Professions Officer (HPO) pay tables.

konular.baslik: Reserve and Guard Retirement Points: Earning a Qualifying Year
konular.ozet: Reserve and National Guard members earn retirement eligibility by accumulating at least 50 retirement points in each of 20 qualifying years (anniversary years); points come from membership (15 per year automatically), drill periods (1 point per drill, 4 per standard weekend), active duty service (1 point per day), and authorized correspondence or online courses. Retirement pay does not begin at age 60 by default, but post-2008 active duty periods can reduce the start age below 60 at a rate of 3 months per 90-day activation, to a floor of age 50. Members can track their retirement points through their branch's Personnel system or on the DFAS retiree portal.

konular.baslik: Family Care Plan (FCP): Required Planning for Single Parents and Dual-Military Couples
konular.ozet: A Family Care Plan is mandatory for single-parent service members and dual-military couples with dependents; it designates a guardian for any child under 19 or dependent adult during deployments or field duty, and must be certified by the unit commander on DA Form 5305 within 30 days of counseling. The plan must be recertified at least annually and updated any time the named guardian or family situation changes; a soldier whose FCP is inadequate or missing may be counseled on voluntary or involuntary separation. The FCP does not override existing court-ordered custody arrangements and requires supporting documents such as a power of attorney, DEERS enrollment proof, and any relevant custody orders.

konular.baslik: Joint Spouse / Married Couples Assignment Program: Co-Location Requests
konular.ozet: The Married Army Couples Program (MACP) and equivalent programs in other branches allow enrolled dual-military couples to request co-located assignments within a 50-mile radius or one-hour drive of each other; enrollment requires both spouses to sign a request with their branch personnel office and submit an official marriage certificate. When one enrolled member receives reassignment orders, the other is automatically considered for the same location, but co-location is a consideration — not a guarantee — and both positions must match the members' skills and career progression requirements. Each branch administers its own program; cross-branch coordination for couples in different services is possible but more complex.

konular.baslik: Post-9/11 GI Bill Monthly Housing Allowance (MHA)
konular.ozet: Post-9/11 GI Bill recipients attending in-person classes receive a Monthly Housing Allowance based on the DoD BAH rate for an E-5 with dependents at the zip code of their school, prorated by enrollment rate and benefit tier; students enrolled online-only receive a flat rate of up to $1,169 per month (August 2025–July 2026). MHA is only paid for months of active enrollment at more than half-time and is not paid in the final month if the student completes the term early. Current MHA rates by location are available on the VA education benefits rates page at va.gov.

konular.baslik: Military Pay Garnishment for Child Support and Alimony
konular.ozet: Under 42 U.S.C. § 659, military pay — including base pay, BAH, BAS, and most special pays — can be garnished by DFAS to satisfy court-ordered child support or alimony obligations; the court order must name DFAS as the garnishee and include the member's full name, Social Security number, and branch of service. The Consumer Credit Protection Act caps garnishment at 50% of disposable pay if the member supports another family, or 60% if not — both limits increase by 5 percentage points if the member is more than 12 weeks in arrears. State child support enforcement agencies can submit orders directly to the DFAS Garnishment Law Directorate; the process is separate from voluntary military allotments set up for support.

konular.baslik: Dental Coverage for Military Retirees: FEDVIP Enrollment
konular.ozet: Military retirees and their family members do not receive dental coverage through TRICARE medical plans; instead, they purchase dental insurance through the Federal Employees Dental and Vision Insurance Program (FEDVIP), administered by the Office of Personnel Management (OPM). Enrollment is available during the annual Federal Benefits Open Season or following a qualifying life event; premiums and plan options vary by carrier and coverage tier. Surviving spouses of active-duty service members who died in the line of duty may qualify for the TRICARE Dental Program Survivor Benefit, under which TRICARE pays the full monthly premium.

konular.baslik: MHS GENESIS Patient Portal: Online Health Record Access for Military Beneficiaries
konular.ozet: MHS GENESIS is the Military Health System's secure online portal providing approximately 9.5 million beneficiaries — including active duty, retirees, and family members — with 24/7 access to their medical records, lab results, radiology reports, and clinical notes. Through the portal, users can manage appointments, message their care team, and request prescription refills from any internet-connected device. Access requires either a Common Access Card (CAC) or a DS Logon credential; the portal is available at patientportal.mhsgenesis.health.mil.

VA operates approximately 250 residential rehabilitation programs at around 120 sites nationwide — including Alaska and Hawaii — providing 24-hour structured care for veterans with PTSD, depression, substance use disorder, and co-occurring conditions, with capacity for more than 6,500 veterans at a time. Typical program stays are around six weeks, though length varies by clinical need, and most programs also address employment readiness, housing stability, and life-skills support. Veterans must be enrolled in VA health care to be eligible; ask your VA primary care provider or mental health team for a referral.

konular.baslik: TRICARE Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA) Coverage for Autism Spectrum Disorder
konular.ozet: TRICARE covers Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA) therapy for all beneficiaries diagnosed with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) through the TRICARE Comprehensive Autism Care Demonstration, with no age limit and no annual or lifetime cap on covered services as long as care is clinically necessary. The access process involves five steps: obtaining an ASD diagnosis from an approved provider, obtaining a referral and pre-authorization (issued in six-month increments), completing four baseline outcome measures, and working with an assigned autism services navigator. Standard TRICARE plan cost-shares apply; visit tricare.mil/autism or contact your regional contractor for provider lookup.

konular.baslik: VA Hepatitis C Treatment: Free Direct-Acting Antivirals for Eligible Veterans
konular.ozet: VA provides direct-acting antiviral (DAA) treatments for hepatitis C at no cost to eligible enrolled veterans, achieving cure rates of 75–93% across patient groups and making VA one of the largest HCV treatment programs in the world. VA has cured more than 100,000 veterans of hepatitis C, and research shows treated veterans are 84% less likely to develop liver cancer compared to untreated veterans. Veterans enrolled in VA health care who have or suspect they have hepatitis C should ask their VA primary care provider for a hepatitis C screening test and referral to the VA's Viral Hepatitis program.

konular.baslik: VA Disability Effective Date and Retroactive Back Pay
konular.ozet: VA disability compensation begins on the effective date — generally the later of the date VA receives the claim or the date the disability arose; if a veteran files within one year of separation from service, the effective date can be as early as the day after separation. For rating increases, VA backdates the increase to the earliest date the worsening is documented, provided the veteran requests the increase within one year of that worsening. Retroactive back pay is the total of monthly compensation from the effective date to the first payment, and can be substantial for claims with long processing times or retroactive separation dates.

konular.baslik: VA-Accredited Claims Agents and Attorneys: Paid Help with Disability Claims
konular.ozet: Veterans may hire VA-accredited attorneys or claims agents to assist with disability claims and appeals; unlike VSOs (which are free), accredited representatives may charge fees, but only after VA issues an initial decision on the claim. Fee agreements must be filed with VA, and fees exceeding 20% of past-due benefits require VA approval — fees above 33⅓% are presumed unreasonable and subject to review. To find an accredited representative, search the VA's official roster at va.gov/ogc/apps/accreditation/; accreditation is obtained through VA's Office of General Counsel and does not imply VA endorsement of the representative.

konular.baslik: Homeless Veteran Reintegration Program (HVRP): Job Training and Placement
konular.ozet: The Homeless Veteran Reintegration Program (HVRP), administered by the Department of Labor's Veterans' Employment and Training Service, is the only federal program focused exclusively on employment of homeless veterans — providing career exploration, job readiness training, skills development, and job placement targeting high-demand occupations. HVRP grants are awarded to nonprofit organizations, local workforce boards, and community groups, not directly to veterans; eligible veterans access services by connecting with a local HVRP grantee in their area. Contact the nearest American Job Center (careeronestop.org) or call the National Call Center for Homeless Veterans at 877-424-3838 to locate nearby HVRP services.

konular.baslik: VA Pension vs. VA Disability Compensation: Key Differences
konular.ozet: VA disability compensation is tax-free and based on a service-connected disability rating (0–100%), with no income or net worth limits; VA pension is taxable, need-based, and available only to low-income wartime veterans who are age 65 or older, permanently disabled, in a nursing home, or receiving SSDI — regardless of whether the disability is service-connected. A veteran eligible for both benefits will receive only the higher of the two amounts, not both simultaneously. The key distinction: compensation requires a connection between a disability and military service; pension requires wartime service and financial need.

konular.baslik: State Veterans Benefits: Property Tax Exemptions, Tuition Waivers, and More
konular.ozet: All 50 states and U.S. territories offer veterans benefits beyond federal programs, commonly including property tax exemptions (some states exempt 100% of home value for veterans rated 100% permanent and total), state education tuition waivers for dependents of disabled or deceased veterans, and combat-zone bonus pay. There is no single federal database of all state benefits, but VA published a comprehensive state-by-state property tax guide in April 2025 covering every state and territory, available at news.va.gov. The authoritative starting point for any state's programs is the state Department of Veterans Affairs or veterans services agency, accessible through the VA Regional Office map at benefits.va.gov/benefits/offices.asp.

konular.baslik: BAH Rate Lookup: How to Find Your Exact Monthly Allowance
konular.ozet: The official Basic Allowance for Housing rate lookup tool is hosted by the Defense Travel Management Office (DTMO) at travel.dod.mil; it requires three inputs — pay grade, dependency status (with or without dependents), and duty station ZIP code — and returns the exact monthly BAH dollar amount for that location. Rates are updated annually on January 1 based on DTMO surveys of median rental costs in each Military Housing Area, and a downloadable PDF of all 2025 rates by location and pay grade is also available on the DTMO site. The lookup tool is the authoritative source for current rates; rate changes do not affect existing BAH amounts already in payment due to the rate protection rule under 37 U.S.C. § 403(b)(6).

INA § 319(e) allows eligible spouses of U.S. citizen service members to complete the entire naturalization process — including the interview and oath ceremony — at a U.S. Embassy or Consulate abroad, without ever traveling to the United States. Time abroad on official military orders may count as both continuous residence and physical presence in the United States for naturalization purposes, eliminating the standard U.S. residency requirement entirely. This is distinct from INA § 319(b), which also waives U.S. residency requirements but requires the applicant to appear in the U.S. for the oath; § 319(e) removes even that requirement for qualifying military spouses.

konular.baslik: VA Grant and Per Diem (GPD) Transitional Housing
konular.ozet: The VA Grant and Per Diem program funds community-based organizations nationwide to provide transitional housing, case management, and supportive services to homeless Veterans. Veterans do not apply directly to VA; they connect with local GPD grantee organizations. Call the National Call Center for Homeless Veterans at 877-424-3838 (24/7, free) to locate services near you.

konular.baslik: VA Home Loan Eligibility for National Guard and Reserve
konular.ozet: National Guard and Selected Reserve members can qualify for a VA home loan with 6 creditable years of service — currently serving or honorably discharged/retired — as an alternative to the standard 90-day active-duty requirement. National Guard members activated under Title 10 for 90+ days (at least 30 consecutive) also qualify if their DD214 shows the applicable USC sections (316, 502, 503, 504, or 505). Obtain a Certificate of Eligibility (COE) through eBenefits or your VA-approved lender.

Service members in designated combat zones receive an automatic IRS deadline extension of 180 days after leaving the zone, plus any days remaining before the original deadline — with no penalties or interest accruing during this period. The extension covers filing and payment of income taxes, estimated tax payments, IRA contributions, Tax Court petitions, and IRS collection deadlines. Spouses receive the same extensions whether filing jointly or separately, with limited exceptions for service members hospitalized in the U.S.

TRICARE covers virtual care — including office visits and mental health services — at the same cost-share as in-person care, with costs varying by plan type. Available tools include the MHS GENESIS Patient Portal for secure messaging and records, the MHS Nurse Advice Line (24/7, phone/chat/video), and Quick Care Connect for virtual urgent care at select locations. Active duty service members must have a referral for all virtual health appointments, just as for in-person visits.

konular.baslik: Installation Family Support Centers (ACS / FFSC / AFRC)
konular.ozet: Every military installation operates a branch-specific family support center — Army Community Service (ACS), Fleet and Family Support Center (FFSC) for Navy and Marines, and Airman and Family Readiness Center (AFRC) for Air Force — providing free in-person services such as deployment and relocation counseling, financial readiness, emergency relief access, employment assistance, and exceptional family member support. These on-base centers complement MilitaryOneSource (phone/online) and are open to all active duty, Guard/Reserve on orders, and their families. Use the Military Installations tool at installations.militaryonesource.mil to find your local center.

konular.baslik: VA Spinal Cord Injuries and Disorders (SCI/D) Program
konular.ozet: VA operates 25 Spinal Cord Injury and Disorders (SCI/D) hub centers and 120 designated spoke facilities providing lifelong, comprehensive care — including primary and specialty care, inpatient rehabilitation, mental health, home care, and assistive technology — for veterans with any spinal cord injury or disorder, whether service-connected or not. Active duty service members are also eligible. To access care, contact the nearest VA SCI/D Center or VA health care facility.

Military retirement pay is adjusted annually based on the Consumer Price Index for Urban Wage Earners and Clerical Workers (CPI-W), matching the Social Security COLA. Retirees under the Final Pay and High-36 plans receive the full CPI-W adjustment; retirees under the CSB/REDUX plan receive COLA minus 1 percentage point each year until age 62, when pay is recalculated to the High-36 level and the reduced-COLA formula resumes. SBP annuitants receive the same COLA as the underlying retirement plan.

konular.baslik: VA HISA Grant (Home Improvements and Structural Alterations)
konular.ozet: The VA Home Improvements and Structural Alterations (HISA) grant funds medically necessary home modifications for veterans with service-connected or compensable disabilities — up to $6,800 lifetime for service-connected (or non-service-connected with 50%+ rating) and $2,000 for other veterans. Covered modifications include permanent ramps, accessible bathroom fixtures, widened doorways, and plumbing/electrical work for home medical equipment; portable ramps, hot tubs, and routine maintenance are excluded. Apply through your local VA Prosthetic and Sensory Aids Service using a VA physician prescription and VA Form 10-0103.
konular.kaynak_adi: VA Prosthetic and Sensory Aids Service

konular.baslik: Federal and State Taxes on Military Retirement Pay
konular.ozet: Standard military retirement pay based on age or length of service is federally taxable income and reported as a pension on Form 1040; however, military disability retirement pay and VA disability compensation may be partially or fully excluded from federal taxes. At the state level, more than 30 states fully exempt military retirement pay from state income tax, while others offer partial exemptions or full taxation — the MyArmyBenefits state tax table is updated annually. VA benefits such as disability compensation and DIC are generally non-taxable at both the federal and state level.
